title: Project tsconfig.json should mirror shipping-service include (cover tests + __mocks__/) so nx typecheck catches them
content:
In Lynk Nx-monorepo services, `projects/<service>/tsconfig.json` is the IDE/typecheck/test-target config. Its `include` should match shipping-service / warehousing-service:

```json
"include": [
  "src/**/*.ts",
  "jest.config.cjs",
  "**/__test__/**/*",
  "**/__mocks__/**/*",
  "src/**/*.test.ts",
  "src/**/*.testutil.ts",
  "src/**/*.d.ts"
]
```

**Why:** the nx `typecheck` target runs `npx tsc -b --noEmit` from `{projectRoot}`, and `jest.config.cjs` typically uses `ts-jest { diagnostics: false }`. If tests/testutils/`__mocks__/` are excluded here, type errors in those files are silently skipped at typecheck *and* at jest runtime — only eslint catches them, and only via a separate `tsconfig.eslint.json`.

**How to apply:** the *narrow* include (tests excluded) belongs in `tsconfig.lib.json` (build target only). Don't repeat it in `tsconfig.json`. When migrating a flat-repo service into the monorepo, expect the broader include to surface pre-existing test-side type drift that the old `tsconfig.test.json`-via-references setup was hiding — budget time to fix those fixtures in the same PR.

title: Cross-`@invenco/*` deps in common subpackages go in peerDependencies, not dependencies
content:
In the `common` monorepo, every cross-`@invenco/*` reference inside a subpackage's `package.json` is declared in `peerDependencies` with version `"*"`, never in `dependencies`. Examples: `@invenco/common-application` peers on `common-domain`, `common-interface`, `ddd-core`, `logging-client`, `tracing`; `@invenco/aws-common-infra` peers on `common-application`, `common-gateways`, `common-interface`, `common-domain`, etc.

**Why:** subpackages are themselves published. Putting a sibling `@invenco/*` in `dependencies` ships a bundled copy and risks dual-resolution when the consumer also depends on that package directly. Peer-with-`*` lets the consumer pick the version and avoids two copies in `node_modules` — important the moment the dep ships any runtime artefact (enum, class, symbol).

**How to apply:** when adding a new `@invenco/*` reference to a `common` subpackage, put it in `peerDependencies` with `"*"`. If the workspace itself needs the package installed for lint/typecheck/build/test (i.e. it's an external `@invenco/*` package, not a workspace sibling), add it to root `package.json` `devDependencies` with the actual version range — same pattern `@invenco/eslint-config-invenco-base` uses. `yarn add` (without `--peer`) will land it in the wrong place; reach for `yarn add --peer ...` in the subpackage plus a root devDep for resolution.

**Source:** PR #660 review feedback on ENG-5084 — initial commit landed the new `@invenco/automation-interface` in `dependencies`; reviewer flagged the convention divergence and the dual-resolution risk; fixed in 220b4b4.

title: Datadog version on Lambdas comes from DD_VERSION env, not stack tags.version
content:
In Lynk Lambda services, the `version` field on Datadog logs and traces is sourced from the `DD_VERSION` environment variable on the Lambda function — NOT from the CloudFormation stack's `provider.tags.version`.

**Rule:** Add `DD_VERSION: ${env:GIT_COMMIT_HASH, 'local-dev'}` to `provider.environment` in the service's `serverless.yml` so Datadog's version tracks each deploy.

**When to apply:** Wiring or auditing Datadog observability on any serverless Lambda service, or when the Datadog `version` field is stale/frozen across deploys.

**Why:** `tags.version` only flows to AWS billing/resource tags; the Datadog Lambda extension reads `DD_VERSION` (alongside `DD_ENV`/`DD_SERVICE`) from the function env at invocation time. If `DD_VERSION` is unset, Datadog falls back to a stale baked-in source that never refreshes, so the version field freezes across deploys and silently misleads incident bisection.

**How to apply / gotchas:**
- Use the repo's existing CI-injected SHA var `GIT_COMMIT_HASH` (exported by `invenco-cbt cd deploy` before it shells out to `sls deploy`; already backing `provider.tags.version` and esbuild's `gitCommitHash` global). Do NOT introduce a raw CI var like `CIRCLE_SHA1` even if a ticket suggests it — match the established repo convention.
- Keep the `'local-dev'` fallback so packaging without CI still succeeds.
- During incident response, treat the Datadog version field as unreliable until `aws lambda get-function-configuration --query 'Environment.Variables.DD_VERSION'` confirms it is set on the function.

**Scope (cross-service):** This gap is org-wide — ~11 serverless services set `DD_SERVICE`+`DD_ENV` but none set `DD_VERSION`. There is no central serverless-templating layer in `invenco-cbt cd deploy`, so the fix lands per-service in each `serverless.yml` plus the `service-template`, not in one shared place.

**Confirmed:** shipping-service PR #574 (one-line add) merged; CircleCI green through the stage deploy on the merge commit.

title: Check inline json-rules-engine usage before widening the Workflow aggregate
content:
When a new use case needs rule-based evaluation, check whether json-rules-engine can be invoked directly from the consumer service before adding new enums (WorkflowScopeType, WorkflowDataType, WorkflowTriggerType) and widening Workflow.validate in @invenco/common-domain.

Reference pattern: warehousing-service evaluates JSON rules inline at runtime without going through the Workflow → Rule → Action abstraction. The engine is reachable directly.

Pitfall the rule prevents (ENG-5225 / PR #677): a Foreman-shaped ticket proposed adding WorkflowScopeType.SHIPPING_QUOTE, WorkflowTriggerType.QUOTE_REQUESTED, and widening Workflow.validate to accept WorkflowDataType.QUOTE to unblock ENG-4985 M2's surcharge applicability evaluator. PR landed, addressed three review-agent findings, and even received an APPROVED review — then dave-scott-invenco and ashishtilara stepped in and clarified that the only domain primitive actually needed (the propertyConditionEvaluationAttributes QUOTE_* map) had already landed in #673, and the consumer can call the engine directly. The Workflow widening was rooted in the flawed assumption that json-rules-engine is only reachable via the Workflow/Rule/Action abstraction.

Application: before widening a WorkflowConfigurationMap entry or a *.validate guard list in @invenco/common-domain for a new aggregate scope, search the existing codebase (start with warehousing-service) for an inline usage of the same primitive. If one exists, that is the reference implementation — extending the aggregate is dead weight that locks the consumer into the aggregate's lifecycle.

title: shipping-service .gitignore excludes docs/ subdirectories
content:
The repo's `.gitignore` has `docs/*` followed by `!docs/*.md`. That negation only matches top-level `.md` files — any subdirectory of `docs/` (e.g. `docs/runbooks/`) is silently ignored, even if it contains tracked-looking `.md` files. Symptoms: `git add` reports nothing untracked but `git check-ignore -v` shows the file matching `docs/*`.

When adding docs/runbooks, drop them directly under `docs/` (flat) or extend the negation. Don't create `docs/<subdir>/file.md` and assume git will pick it up.

title: Run foreman type-check gate with `yarn run check`, not `yarn check`
content:
**Rule:** In the foreman repo, run the type-check gate with `yarn run check`, never bare `yarn check`.
**When to apply:** Running the verification gate after editing TypeScript, before committing/pushing.
**Anti-pattern:** `yarn check` invokes Yarn Classic's built-in dependency-integrity verifier (not the package.json `check` script) and prints hundreds of spurious `"X#Y" not installed` lines ending in `Found N errors` — easily misread as a broken build. The real gate is the `check` script: `tsc -p tsconfig.build.json --noEmit && tsc -p tsconfig.json && yarn workspace @foreman/ui typecheck`.
**Example:** `yarn check` → `error Found 789 errors`; `yarn run check` → clean typecheck.

title: Mirror non-continuation Context block order in continuation prompt templates
content:
**Rule:** When adding `{{context:*}}` blocks to a continuation prompt template (`prompts/templates/*-continuation.md`), replicate the non-continuation template's `## Context` block order exactly and append any new block last.
**When to apply:** Editing a `*-continuation.md` template, or adding a context block consumed by both the initial and continuation templates.
**Anti-pattern:** Reordering, interleaving, or dropping the shared context blocks (the continuation templates originally kept only `git-state`) forces the worker to re-discover task/PR state and breaks prompt-cache prefix reuse — costly because the Anthropic cache TTL Foreman runs under is 5 minutes (`src/execution/cost/rates.ts`). Keep the shared prefix identical up to the first intentional divergence (`## Scope`).
**Example:** review-continuation Context = selected-task → task-provider → repo → git-state → pull-request (matches review.md), then prior-checkpoint appended last.

title: Continuation-conditional runner knobs: swap at construction, keep the session key on base tuning
content:
**Rule:** When a runner-config knob is conditional on continuation (first-pass vs. session-resuming dispatch), apply it ONLY where the runner is constructed (`createAgentRunner`); keep the runner-session selector and attempt record on the BASE tuning.

**When to apply:** Adding any continuation-conditional runner knob in Foreman — e.g. `continuationEffort`/`continuationVariant` (ENG-5319), or a future `continuationModel`.

**Anti-pattern:** Passing the continuation flag into the `resolveRunnerConfigForAction` call that feeds the session selector (`attempt-executor.ts` ~line 164). The session key includes `runnerVariant` (= effort/variant), so a continuation would look up a session keyed by the continuation value, never match the base-tuned session created on the first pass, and silently start a fresh session every time — defeating continuation.

**Also:** `retry` is never a continuation — `attempt-executor.ts` excludes `job.action === "retry"` from `getActiveSession`, so `isContinuation` is always false for retry (and first-pass execution). Continuation knobs never affect them.

**Example:** ENG-5319 builds the runner with `continuation: isContinuation` at `attempt-executor.ts` ~line 276 (after the session is resolved), while the line-164 `resolveRunnerConfigForAction` stays `continuation=false` for the selector + attempt record. `runnerForActionAndContinuation` swaps the tuning; `runnerTuningValue` (the session key) stays on the base.

**Reviewing such a change:** (1) Confirm the continuation flag reaches EVERY runner-construction site and NO session-lookup/attempt-record site — e.g. the reviewer-session-cleanup `resolveRunnerConfigForAction` at `attempt-executor.ts` ~line 429 must stay on base, since it keys a `getActiveSession` lookup, not a runner build. (2) Require a continuation-swap picker test per provider: `applyContinuationTuning` duplicates the claude and codex branches verbatim for TS enum narrowing, so a refactor can silently break the untested provider — verify claude, codex, AND opencode each have a swap test, not just a schema-parse test.

title: Assert structured payload fields in prompt-render tests, not just one narrative field
content:
**Rule:** When a prompt template gains a `{{context:*}}` block rendered as a structured JSON section, the render test must assert the payload's structured fields appear (by key, with the deterministic fixture values), not only one human-readable field.
**When to apply:** Adding or changing a structured context block in `prompts/templates/*` together with its assertions in `src/__tests__/prompts.test.ts`.
**Anti-pattern:** Asserting only a narrative field (e.g. `priorPassSummary`) while the structured fields that are the block's actual purpose (e.g. `headSha`, `checksFingerprint`, `mergeState`) go unasserted — a rename or drop in the renderer's return object then passes CI silently and guts the feature. Also assert the defensive fallback branches (empty/placeholder values), not just the happy path.
**Example:** ENG-5318's new continuation tests assert `priorPassSummary` + the summary text but not the checkpoint fingerprint fields; the existing non-continuation review test already models the right shape with `expect(reviewPrompt).toContain('"headSha": "abc123"')` (prompts.test.ts:383).

title: Assert executor-wired createAgentRunner args; the runner mock ignores them
content:
**Rule:** When wiring a runner-config value through `AttemptExecutor`, assert it in `attempt-executor.test.ts` with `expect(createAgentRunner).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({ ... }))` — the `createAgentRunner` mock returns `{ invoke }` and ignores its call args, so a dropped or wrong executor arg leaves every test green while the feature silently no-ops.

**When to apply:** Adding or changing any argument passed to `createAgentRunner` from the executor (model, effort, `continuation`, future knobs like a `continuationModel`).

**Anti-pattern:** Relying only on `resolveRunnerConfigForAction`/`runnerForActionAndContinuation` unit tests. They verify config resolution in isolation, not that the executor passes the resolved value through — the exact gap a reviewer flagged on ENG-5319.

**Example:** ENG-5319 guards `continuation: isContinuation` (attempt-executor.ts ~276) with two tests: `continuation: false` for a first-pass attempt and `continuation: true` when an active session with a `nativeSessionId` is seeded. Both fail under mutation (drop the arg). To exercise the true branch, seed `db.runnerSessions.createSession({ ...selector, role: "implementation", isActive: true, nativeSessionId })`.

title: Assert individual serialized field keys, not just a summary marker
content:
**Rule:** When a test asserts on rendered/serialized structured output, pin each individual field key (and its deterministic value where stable), not just one representative summary/marker substring.
**When to apply:** Testing any function that emits a JSON or structured block consumed downstream — prompt context blocks, API payloads, serialized records.
**Anti-pattern:** Asserting only one field (e.g. a summary string) lets a silent rename or drop of the sibling keys pass the test green while the downstream consumer loses the data it depends on; the regression surfaces only in production.
**Example:** the prior-checkpoint continuation tests assert `headSha`/`mergeState` values plus the presence of `latestReviewSummaryId`, `latestConversationCommentId`, `reviewThreadsFingerprint`, `checksFingerprint`, and `recordedAt`, not just `priorPassSummary`.

title: Exercise a missing-referent branch via FK ON DELETE SET NULL in foreman tests
content:
**Rule:** To exercise a "referent missing" / not-found fallback branch in a foreman test when the column is a foreign key declared `ON DELETE SET NULL`, insert the row with a valid referent, then delete the referent and let the FK null the column — the mapper stringifies NULL to a lookup id that misses.
**When to apply:** Covering fallback branches that read a `*_attempt_id`-style FK column where FK enforcement is on (`PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON`), so a fabricated id cannot be inserted directly.
**Anti-pattern:** Upserting a row with a made-up `sourceAttemptId` (the FK rejects it), or skipping the branch as untestable.
**Example:** `db.database.sqlite.prepare("DELETE FROM execution_attempt WHERE id = ?").run(attempt.id)` after seeding the checkpoint nulls `review_checkpoint.source_attempt_id`; `getAttempt("null")` then throws `attempt_not_found`, so the prompt renders `(prior pass summary unavailable)`.

title: Foreman tickets can lag the codebase — verify before implementing
content:
**Rule:** Before implementing a Foreman ticket, grep the codebase for the behavior it describes — the feature may already exist, so scope the change to the genuine gaps (missing buckets, absent tests, edge cases) instead of re-building it from the ticket's step-by-step instructions.
**When to apply:** Any execution task whose ticket claims a capability is "missing" or hands you literal build-from-scratch steps, especially in a fast-moving repo where a foundational commit may have landed the feature after the ticket was drafted.
**Anti-pattern:** Following the ticket's Instructions verbatim and adding a duplicate/conflicting implementation (e.g. a second startup validator) that fights existing code and balloons the diff.
**Example:** ENG-5321 asked to add Linear label/state startup validation; `validateStartup()` already existed and was wired into `foreman serve` — the real work was covering the `deployable` state bucket, collecting all missing names, and adding the missing unit tests.

title: Inlining schema enums into prompt fragments collides with not.toContain token guards
content:
**Rule:** When inlining a schema or discriminated-union enum into a shared prompt fragment, retarget any sibling prompt tests that assert `not.toContain("<token>")` at the instruction *phrase* rather than the bare enum token.
**When to apply:** Any Foreman change that renders a Zod schema / mutation-type enum into `prompts/fragments/*` consumed by multiple worker templates (execution/review/reviewer/retry/deployment/consolidation and their continuations).
**Anti-pattern:** Leaving `expect(prompt).not.toContain("create_pull_request")`-style guards in place — the inlined schema now lists every mutation type in every prompt, so the bare token appears everywhere and the leanness assertion breaks (or, if loosened, silently stops guarding anything).
**Example:** ENG-5320 inlined the agent-result schema into `output-validator.md`; `create_pull_request` / `submit_pull_request_review` then appeared in review/reviewer continuations, so the guards were retargeted to "Use `create_pull_request` whenever" and "use a `submit_pull_request_review` mutation".

title: Run `yarn run check`, not `yarn check`, to typecheck foreman
content:
**Rule:** In foreman, invoke the verify gate as `yarn run check` (and `yarn run test`/`yarn run build`); bare `yarn check` triggers yarn v1's built-in node_modules integrity command, not the package.json `check` script.
**When to apply:** Any execution/review/reviewer/retry pass that must confirm a foreman change typechecks — especially since the ticket verify section literally says `yarn check`.
**Anti-pattern:** Reading `yarn check`'s hundreds of "X not installed" lines as real failures, or its exit 0 as a green typecheck. It never ran tsc.
**Example:** `yarn check` -> `Found 789 errors` from the integrity checker; `yarn run check` -> `tsc -p tsconfig.build.json --noEmit && ...` exits 0 clean.

title: Don't flag a missing fixture when inline text and validator share one source
content:
**Rule:** When a refactor inlines text that a validator/CLI also emits, and both render from one shared helper over the same schema object, treat drift as structurally impossible and do not raise "add a committed fixture snapshot" as a gap, even if the ticket suggested a fixture.
**When to apply:** Reviewing a change that replaces a runtime lookup (e.g. a `--help` round-trip) with inlined text sourced from the same function the validator uses.
**Anti-pattern:** Filing a sub-threshold thread asking for a snapshot fixture; it becomes a hard resolver work-unit guarding a non-risk, and a self-referential test that recomputes the schema from the same source cannot catch a divergence that cannot occur.
**Example:** ENG-5320 sources both `agent-result validate --help` and the inlined prompt schema from `renderAgentResultSchemaHelp` over `workerResultSchema` -- single source, so a fixture adds no drift protection.

title: Reviewing foreman: ForemanError throws are by design, not a Lynk Result violation
content:
**Rule:** When reviewing the `foreman` repo, do not apply the Lynk `Result<Happy,Sad>`/never-throw guardrail; foreman is the orchestrator (TypeScript/vitest), not a Lynk DDD domain service, and surfaces failures by throwing `ForemanError`.
**When to apply:** Any review/reviewer pass on foreman code, especially when the review-changes Lynk architecture overlay or lynk-backend review skills tempt you to grep `throw new` and flag it as a Result-discipline breach.
**Anti-pattern:** Flagging `throw new ForemanError(...)` as a Result-type violation. These throws are intentional fail-loud behavior, often validated eagerly at `foreman serve` before the scheduler (e.g. `LinearTaskSystem.validateStartup()` wired at `src/cli.ts`). Swallowing the error would be the real smell, not throwing.
**Example:** ENG-5321 PR #88 hardens `validateStartup()` to throw `linear_state_not_found`/`linear_label_not_found` on a configured-value miss — correct by design.

title: Reviewing foreman PRs: local master lags the fork; take the diff from gh pr diff
content:
**Rule:** Compute a foreman PR review diff from `gh pr diff <n> --repo <fork>` (or against `origin/<base>`), not `git diff master...HEAD`.
**When to apply:** Any reviewer/review pass running inside a foreman worker worktree.
**Anti-pattern:** Trusting the worktree's local `master` ref. It can lag the PR's actual base by 100+ commits, so `git diff master...HEAD` shows a huge phantom diff and an agent can waste a pass reviewing unrelated code.
**Example:** ENG-5321 PR #88 was 2 files (+134/-14), but `git diff master...HEAD` reported 112 files because local master was stale.

title: Feature-flagged nav entries belong in the modern navigation-sidebar, not the legacy nav
content:
**Rule:** In lynk-frontend, add or feature-flag-gate navigation entries in the modern `src/components/navigation-sidebar/` (items.tsx + navigation-sidebar.tsx), not the legacy `src/pages/home/navigation/items.tsx`.
**When to apply:** Any task adding a nav item or gating one behind a feature flag (mirror the `simulation-ui` precedent).
**Anti-pattern:** Editing the legacy nav — it has no feature-flag mechanism and only renders when `useNewUiFeatureFlag()` is off, so the actually-rendered (new-UI) nav won't show the entry. Tickets often cite the legacy file by mistake.
**Example:** ENG-5326 gated a 'Carrier accounts' child under PLATFORM→Shipping; gating a *child* also required adding `featureFlag?` to `NavSubItem`, filtering children in navigation-sidebar.tsx (the existing filter only covered top-level items), and updating navigation-sidebar.test.tsx to skip flag-hidden children.

title: Client-paginated list pages must load all backend pages (fetchAllPages), not just the first
content:
**Rule:** For a shadcn list table that client-paginates/sorts (`manualPagination: false`), load the full result set with `fetchAllPages` over a gateway method that accepts `{ cursor, take }`; if the gateway method lacks those params, add them (mirroring `getCarriers`/`getCarrierServices`) rather than fetching a single page.
**When to apply:** Building or reviewing any list page whose table paginates/sorts client-side over the fetched data.
**Anti-pattern:** Calling the gateway once (`getX(undefined)` + `select: r => r.items`) when the backend is paginated — the table then silently renders only the backend's first page and total/sort run over the truncated set. Don't assume an endpoint can't paginate because the current gateway method omits the params; a `pagination` envelope in the response and paginating sibling endpoints are strong signals it does. (Corrects the earlier guidance to fall back to plain `useQuery`.)
**Example:** ENG-5326 review: `getCarrierAccounts` gained `take`/`cursor` and the carrier-account list switched to `fetchAllPages`; a browser test asserts a row from the second backend page renders.

title: Client-side DataTable list pages must load all pages via fetchAllPages
content:
**Rule:** A list page that renders a client-side DataTable (`manualPagination: false` / `manualSorting: false`) must load the FULL dataset first via `fetchAllPages(({ cursor, take }) => gateway.getX({ cursor, take }, { signal }))` — never feed it a single `gateway.getX(...)` response.
**When to apply:** Authoring or reviewing any `src/pages/**/*-list` page that uses `useDataTable` with `manualPagination: false`. Also confirm the gateway method actually accepts `cursor`/`take`; some (e.g. `getCarrierAccounts`) only take filter params and silently return one page, so the page cannot paginate even if it wanted to.
**Anti-pattern:** Fetch one server page and client-paginate over it. The table shows correct-looking pagination/sorting/total, but every row past the backend's default page size is silently invisible — a data-loss-on-display bug. A `testPagination`/`testDataTable` mock that returns all rows in one response masks it (the test passes against the truncating code). Real coverage requires a mock returning `pagination.next` across >=2 pages plus an assertion that a later-page row renders.
**Example:** ENG-5326 carrier-account-list used `getCarrierAccounts(undefined, {signal})` + `manualPagination:false`; siblings carrier-service-list / carrier-list / account-list all wrap the call in `fetchAllPages`.

title: Dedup the first step of a multi-call useGatewayMutation across retries
content:
**Rule:** When one `useGatewayMutation` `mutationFn` chains two or more non-idempotent calls (create A, then create child-of-A), cache the first call's result in a `useRef` and skip re-issuing it on retry.
**When to apply:** Any create/save flow that performs sequential POSTs in a single `mutationFn` where an early step has side effects and a later step can fail.
**Anti-pattern:** react-query re-runs the whole `mutationFn` on retry, so resubmitting after a later-step failure recreates the already-created resource (orphaned duplicate) — and rollback often isn't available (no DELETE), so dedup at the call site.
**Example:** ENG-5326 create page caches the account id in `createdAccountIdRef`; a retry after a failed `createCarrierWeightBracket` only re-runs the bracket call. Covered by a browser test asserting `createCarrierAccount` is called once across a reject-then-resolve retry.

title: Cache created-entity id in a ref to avoid duplicate creation on mutation retry
content:
**Rule:** When a create flow issues multiple sequential, non-idempotent gateway writes inside one `useGatewayMutation`/`useMutation` `mutationFn` (create parent, then create child records — no combined endpoint), cache the id of any already-created entity in a `useRef`: on entry use `ref.current ?? (await createParent()).id` and store it, so a retry after a later call fails re-runs only the failed call instead of creating a duplicate parent.
**When to apply:** Authoring or reviewing a write-side page whose save path calls two or more gateway create methods in sequence and stays on the form on failure (partial write possible). Watch for `mutationFn: async () => { await createA(); await createB(); }` with no idempotency guard.
**Anti-pattern:** Re-running the whole `mutationFn` on retry recreates the parent every time the user resubmits after a later-step failure, leaving orphaned duplicates. Each failure is surfaced via toast, so it is not a silent failure — but it is a silent data-integrity bug (duplicate rows). Full rollback/delete is usually out of scope; the ref-cache is the cheap, in-scope mitigation. Cover it with a retry test: first child call rejects, second resolves, assert the parent create was called exactly once.
**Example:** ENG-5326 carrier-account create did `createCarrierAccount` then `createCarrierWeightBracket`; a bracket failure + resubmit created a second account until a `createdAccountIdRef` was added (fixed in b54510f6, with a retry browser test).

title: Gateway create payloads may need fields the generated *-interface DTO omits
content:
**Rule:** When writing or reviewing a hand-written `*Gateway` create/update method, confirm the request body includes every field the target API requires — the generated `@invenco/*-interface` DTO is NOT authoritative for required-ness and can omit fields the API mandates. If a required field isn't on the DTO and isn't collected by the form yet, inject a safe default in the gateway (with an explanatory comment) and assert it in the gateway unit test.
**When to apply:** Adding a gateway `createX`/`updateX` against a service endpoint, especially when the `*-interface` package is pinned to a branch pre-release that may lag the deployed API. Symptom: the create 400s against DEV even though the TypeScript compiles.
**Anti-pattern:** Trusting `Partial<XDTO>` as the complete request schema and shipping a create that omits an API-required field — it typechecks and unit-tests green (mocked gateways never hit the real schema) but 400s against the live service. Verify required fields against the running API, not just the type.
**Example:** ENG-5326 `createCarrierAccount` 400'd on every create until the gateway sent `authorization: {}` (required by `POST /v1/carrier_accounts`, absent from `CarrierAccountDTO`); fixed in 5483e495 with a matching gateway-test assertion.

title: Retarget bare-token prompt leanness guards when a base change inlines the schema
content:
**Rule:** When merging a base change that inlines a schema or content blob into worker prompts, retarget any `expect(...).not.toContain("<bareToken>")` prompt-leanness guard to the surrounding instruction phrase — the token now legitimately appears inside the inlined content, so the bare-token guard silently false-fails.
**When to apply:** Resolving a merge (or authoring leanness guards) in `src/__tests__/prompts.test.ts` after a base change inlined the agent-result schema, whose mutation enum lists tokens like `create_pull_request` and `submit_pull_request_review`.
**Anti-pattern:** Keeping `not.toContain("create_pull_request")` once the schema is inlined — it fails even though the continuation prompt correctly omits the PR-creation *instruction*. The fix is to assert omission of the instruction phrase (`not.toContain("Use \`create_pull_request\` whenever")`, `not.toContain("use a \`submit_pull_request_review\` mutation")`).
**Example:** ENG-5318 merge with `4e4793b`: the review/reviewer-continuation leanness guards had to move from bare schema tokens to instruction phrases while keeping the restored-context-block assertions.

title: Review the merge resolution, not the base's changes, when a base-merge lands on an open PR
content:
**Rule:** When a continuation review finds the new head is a merge of the base branch into the PR branch, review the merge *resolution*, not the base's already-merged changes: (1) confirm the PR's net diff vs base still contains only the feature's files; (2) for each conflicted or overlapping file, diff the merge result against BOTH parents (`git diff <branch-parent> <merge>` and `git diff <base-parent> <merge>`) to confirm both intents survived; (3) confirm auto-merged feature files still contain the feature's code; (4) typecheck and run the affected suites on the merged tree.
**When to apply:** A continuation reviewer/review pass where the new head SHA is a merge commit (two parents: prior branch tip + base tip), especially when the merge message lists `# Conflicts:`.
**Anti-pattern:** Re-reviewing every file the merge pulled in from base wastes the pass (they were already reviewed on base); conversely, rubber-stamping the merge without diffing against both parents misses a mis-resolved conflict that silently drops one side's tests or behaviour.
**Example:** ENG-5318 PR #85 merged origin/master with a conflict in prompts.test.ts; diffing 64e9448 against both parents showed master's inline-schema test rework and ENG-5318's checkpoint tests both preserved, the PR net diff stayed at the 5 feature files, and typecheck + prompts/attempt-executor suites were green.

title: Independently-saved editor cards re-seeding from a shared query rely on React Query structural sharing
content:
**Rule:** When a detail page renders multiple independently-saved editor widgets (one card/row per item) that each buffer local edits in state and re-seed from props via `useEffect([config])`, and each save invalidates ONE shared React Query key, that design only preserves OTHER cards' unsaved edits because React Query's default `structuralSharing: true` keeps unchanged items' object references stable (an unchanged sibling's `config` prop reference doesn't change, so its re-seed effect doesn't fire). Preserve that invariant.
**When to apply:** Reviewing or authoring a page with N per-item editor cards that share a query key and each save+invalidate independently (e.g. carrier-account weight-bracket cards). Trigger: a `useEffect` that calls `setState(fromProps)` / resets a dirty flag, keyed on a prop derived from a list query that sibling saves invalidate.
**Anti-pattern:** Anything that breaks per-item reference stability silently resets a sibling card's in-progress edits when another card saves: `structuralSharing: false`, deep-cloning items when deriving per-card props (`.map(x => ({ ...x }))`), or giving cards a `key`/identity that churns on refetch. Single-card tests won't catch it — add a test that edits card B, saves card A, and asserts card B's unsaved input survives. If cards must mutate truly independently, prefer per-card query keys.
**Example:** ENG-5326 carrier-account edit renders a `CarrierWeightBracketCard` per bracket; each re-seeds via `useEffect([config])` and saves into the shared `['carrierAccounts', id, 'carrierWeightBrackets']` key — correct only because structural sharing keeps unchanged brackets' `bracketsConfig` references stable (commit 3a88f9b2).

title: shadcn/radix Select item values cannot be empty strings — use a sentinel for all/none options
content:
**Rule:** In a shadcn/radix `Select`, never give a `SelectItem` `value=""` (radix forbids it — empty string is reserved for the cleared/placeholder state). For an "all"/"none"/"any"/default option, define a sentinel constant (e.g. `const ALL_SERVICES_VALUE = 'all-services'`), use it as the item value, and map it back when reading (`selection === ALL_SERVICES_VALUE ? null : selection`).
**When to apply:** Building or reviewing any shadcn `Select` that needs an option representing "no specific value" (null / all / catch-all). Recurs because this repo is mid Ant Design -> shadcn migration.
**Anti-pattern:** `<SelectItem value="">All</SelectItem>` or treating `''` as a real selectable choice — radix throws at runtime ("A <Select.Item /> must have a value prop that is not an empty string"). An empty `value` on the root `Select` is only valid as the uncontrolled placeholder state, never as an item value.
**Example:** ENG-5326 AddCarrierServiceSheet uses `ALL_SERVICES_VALUE` for the account-level default option and maps it to `carrierServiceId: null` on submit.

title: A maintainer's 'marking ready' FYI/open-question comment is not an agent change request
content:
**Rule:** In a continuation review pass, treat a maintainer's own 'marking ready for review' comment — dependency notes and conditional open questions included — as context for human reviewers, not as a directed change request; do not implement it speculatively.
**When to apply:** The current PR head is maintainer-authored, there are no current-head review summaries and no unresolved threads, CI is green, and the only post-head activity is the author's own FYI/open-question comment.
**Anti-pattern:** Reading 'open question: does the backend dedupe? no client-side guard exists' as a defect and adding code to a maintainer-owned, in-flight branch — overstepping and risking conflicts. The author scoped it explicitly ('no FE change needed if the backend handles it').
**Example:** ENG-5326 PR #1108: head advanced 13 maintainer commits past the agent's last push, all CI green, zero open threads; the sole post-head comment was the author marking ready with a backend-dedupe open question → no_action_needed.

title: Defer large base-integration merge conflicts on a maintainer-owned, active branch
content:
**Rule:** When a continuation review pass finds the PR CONFLICTING but the branch is human-maintainer-owned and actively developed AND the base (e.g. master) has advanced substantially across shared infra the maintainer's new code depends on, defer the base-integration to the maintainer instead of force-pushing a large speculative merge — even though the generic rules direct conflict resolution.
**When to apply:** mergeState CONFLICTING; PR head is far ahead of the agent's last commit with many human-authored commits (often already 'ready for review'); the base diff is large and touches shared components/gateways the maintainer's agent-unreviewed code uses; there is no other current-head review feedback you can safely act on.
**Anti-pattern:** Checking out the PR head and merging a 100+ file base advance, then pushing onto the human's ready-for-review branch — a clean *textual* merge can still semantically break the maintainer's new code (base changing a shared prop/API), which the agent can't reliably validate, and it intrudes on the author's normal final step of integrating base themselves. (A small, single-file, agent-owned conflict is still fine to resolve.)
**Example:** ENG-5326 PR #1108: only routes.tsx conflicted textually, but master had advanced 133 files (billing cutover, returns, order-edit) touching data-table/forms/gateways the maintainer's new carrier-account code uses -> deferred. Outcome (confirmed): the maintainer later merged master into the branch themselves (PR became MERGEABLE) with no agent intervention, validating the deferral.

title: Harden external-config startup validation: bound the page, cover every bucket, report all misses
content:
**Rule:** When startup-validating configured values against an external system, fetch the comparison set with an explicit page bound (or a server-side `name: { in: [...] }` filter), enumerate every config bucket, and collect all misses into a single throw.
**When to apply:** Writing or reviewing any "confirm configured X exists in external system Y" check — especially a client-side membership test against a paginated API (e.g. Linear `issueLabels` / team `states`).
**Anti-pattern:** (a) Unbounded/default-paged fetch (Linear defaults to 50) silently truncates the available set, so a validly-configured value beyond the page yields a FALSE not-found in large workspaces — bound it (foreman uses `first: 250`, matching its sibling label queries) or filter server-side by the exact configured names. (b) Hand-listing config buckets drifts: foreman's loop omitted `states.deployable`, leaving a silent validation hole for that bucket — iterate the full set (or derive it) so every bucket is covered. (c) Throwing on the first miss makes the operator fix one typo, restart, then hit the next — compute `missing = configured.filter(x => !available.has(x))` and throw once naming every missing value.
**Example:** ENG-5321 / PR #88 hardened `validateStartup` in `src/tasking/impl/linear-task-system.ts`: `issueLabels(first: 250)`, added the `deployable` bucket to the state set, and replaced both first-miss throws with filter-then-throw-all for `linear_label_not_found` / `linear_state_not_found`.

title: Write the created record into the React Query cache (setQueryData) so it immediately carries its id for follow-up edits
content:
**Rule:** For a create-then-edit flow where a freshly-created entity must carry its server id on the very next render (e.g. inline rows/cards that create on first save then update on the next), write the mutation's returned record into the React Query cache in `onSuccess` via `queryClient.setQueryData(key, list => upsertById(list, saved))` instead of invalidate-and-refetch. The next render reads the id straight from the cache, so a quick second save updates rather than POSTing a duplicate.
**When to apply:** Any `useGatewayMutation`/`useMutation` whose result must be visible before a refetch could resolve — create-then-immediately-edit, per-row/per-card saves keyed on an entity id derived from a list query.
**Anti-pattern:** Relying on `invalidateQueries`/`linkedQuery` alone leaves a lag window — the new id only arrives after the refetch resolves, so a fast follow-up save re-creates a duplicate. A `useRef` holding the created id works but is a workaround; the `setQueryData` upsert is cleaner and keeps the cache authoritative. Guard the upsert (replace-if-id-present, else append).
**Example:** ENG-5326 `useCarrierAccount.saveBrackets` writes the saved CarrierWeightBracket into `['carrierAccounts', id, 'carrierWeightBrackets']` in `onSuccess` (replace-by-id or append), so a just-created config immediately has its id — replacing the earlier per-card `createdBracketIdRef` workaround.

title: table-fixed tables need min-width or mobile headers overprint
content:
**Rule:** For a `table-fixed w-full` table that has a flexible (`<col>` with no width) column, set a `min-w-[...]` on the `<Table>` (≥ the sum of the fixed colgroup widths) so the `overflow-x-auto` container scrolls instead of collapsing the flexible column to ~0 at narrow viewports.
**When to apply:** Adding or editing a `table-fixed` table in the foreman UI (e.g. the overview Needs Review / Recent Attempts tables), or whenever table columns collide/overprint at mobile widths.
**Anti-pattern:** Trusting `overflow-x-auto` alone. Body cells use `truncate` + `overflow-hidden` so they clip cleanly, but `TableHead` is `whitespace-nowrap` and *unclipped* — when the flexible column squishes, the header text overflows into its neighbour and overprints (the classic "PuMdRequ" collision). The shared `overflow-x-auto` does not help because `w-full` pins the table to the container width.
**Example:** ENG-5370 — `review-table` got `min-w-[36rem]`, `attempts-table` `min-w-[48rem]`; measured header text overflow dropped from 94px/58px to 0 at 390px.

title: Verify foreman UI via worktree vite dev (/api proxy to running host)
content:
**Rule:** To visually verify foreman UI changes against real data, start the worktree's own dev server (`yarn workspace @foreman/ui dev --port <free> --strictPort`) and point agent-browser at it. Vite proxies `/api` to `FOREMAN_API_ORIGIN` (default `http://127.0.0.1:8765`), so your local UI renders the running host's live data without standing up a backend.
**When to apply:** Any foreman UI execution/review task that needs visual or behavioral verification (screenshots, layout/overlap audits via `agent-browser eval`) while a Foreman host is already serving the workspace.
**Anti-pattern:** Screenshotting `http://127.0.0.1:8765` directly to check your work — that host serves the *main source* build, not your worktree, so you verify the old UI. Useful inverse: because :8765 is the pre-change build, it doubles as a free before/after baseline.
**Example:** ENG-5370 — same `agent-browser eval` overlap audit on :4180 (worktree) vs :8765 (baseline) proved the overprint fix (0 vs 94px header overflow at 390px).

title: Long-form table-cell content belongs in a detail Sheet; line-clamp needs whitespace-normal in DataTable cells
content:
**Rule:** In the Foreman UI, render long-form or markdown content as a clamped preview in the DataTable cell and surface the full body in a row-click detail Sheet (useSearchParams + onRowClick + <Sheet> + a per-entity DetailSheet), mirroring Attempts/Tasks. When clamping a cell with line-clamp-N, also add `whitespace-normal`.
**When to apply:** Adding, editing, or reviewing a `ui/src/pages/*/columns.tsx` column that holds multi-line text, a markdown body, or any long-form string, or its detail Sheet.
**Anti-pattern:** Dumping the full body in the cell balloons row height and table width, pushing right-hand columns off-screen. `line-clamp-N` without `whitespace-normal` silently shows only one line, because the shared `TableCell` (ui/src/components/ui/table.tsx) defaults to `whitespace-nowrap`.
**Review note:** These Sheets drive `open` off the URL id but pass a nullable resolved object for content (Tasks passes a nullable `bucket`; Attempts/Learnings resolve `find(...) ?? null`), so a Sheet can open "empty" on a stale or deep-linked id before data loads — this is the intentional house convention, not a missing not-found state; do not flag it.
**Example:** ENG-5371 learnings 'Learning' column -> `line-clamp-2 max-w-72 whitespace-normal` preview + `LearningDetailSheet` opened via a `learningId` URL param.

title: Isolate agent-browser with AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION when verifying UI during Foreman execution
content:
**Rule:** When verifying a UI with agent-browser during Foreman task execution, run every command under an isolated session via `AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION=<name>` (or `--session`). The default session can be driven concurrently by another process (e.g. the running pilot), so separate open/eval/screenshot calls otherwise land on a different tab/port.
**When to apply:** Any agent-browser-based visual or DOM-measurement verification while executing inside Foreman.
**Anti-pattern:** Using the default session -> evals jump between pages/ports across commands (e.g. reading a different page's columns or stale/unmodified code), producing nonsense overflow/visibility numbers and wasted iterations. Also avoid `agent-browser close --all`, which can close a concurrent process's session.
**Example:** ENG-5371 — `export AGENT_BROWSER_SESSION=eng5371` before set-viewport/open/screenshot/eval gave stable readings; the default session flip-flopped between the task's dev server (4173) and another instance.

title: Shared shadcn container visual changes: verify backdrop assumptions across all consumers
content:
**Rule:** When reviewing a change to a shared `ui/src/components/ui/*` container that adds a backdrop-dependent visual effect (scroll shadow, edge mask, overlay tint), verify the color/contrast assumption against EVERY consumer's wrapper, not just the one shown in the diff.
**When to apply:** A shared shadcn/ui component gains a CSS effect whose correctness depends on the surface it sits on (e.g. a cover/shadow gradient tuned to a specific bg-* token).
**Anti-pattern:** Approving after checking a single page; the effect then mismatches on other pages whose wrapper uses a different background, leaving visible edge bands or invisible shadows.
**Example:** ENG-5370's `scroll-shadow-x` cover is tuned to `bg-card/75` — correct only because all four foreman table surfaces wrap in `bg-card/75` (overview `TableSectionShell` + `DataTableShell`).

title: foreman yarn test/lint carry pre-existing failures unrelated to scoped changes
content:
**Rule:** When verifying a scoped change in foreman, judge it by your own diff, not by whole-suite green — both `yarn test` and `yarn workspace @foreman/ui lint` carry pre-existing failures unrelated to most changes.
**When to apply:** After a small/isolated edit (e.g. one UI lib file) you run the ticket's full verification suite and see red in files you never touched.
**Anti-pattern:** Blocking, or "fixing" the unrelated red and adding churn. The backend infra suites fail independently of UI/library edits — `src/workspace/__tests__/git-worktrees.test.ts` (5s timeouts under load), `src/__tests__/foreman-version.test.ts` (git origin-state flakiness), and ENOTEMPTY temp-dir races — and `lint` reports standing errors (e.g. `react-hooks/set-state-in-effect` in `ui/src/pages/settings/index.tsx` and other UI files).
**How to confirm yours vs pre-existing:** stash just your diff (`git stash push <files>`) and re-run the failing suites — identical failures = pre-existing; or scope lint/tests to the changed file/area. foreman has no CI workflow, so nothing else gates this for you.
**Example:** A one-file `ui/src/lib/format.ts` change showed 13 `yarn test` failures + 12 lint errors, all reproduced identically on the stashed baseline, while `yarn run check`, UI typecheck, and UI build passed.

title: Inline generated content into prompts behind a shared source + drift guard
content:
**Rule:** When you remove a CLI/subprocess round-trip by inlining its generated output into a worker/continuation prompt fragment, (a) emit both the inline copy and the original command from ONE shared helper sourced from the canonical schema, bind a guard test to that same generator, and (b) retarget any pre-existing negative test assertions that matched a bare domain token onto the surrounding instruction phrasing.
**When to apply:** Replacing a `--help`/lookup shell turn with inline templated text (e.g. a `{{context:result-schema}}` substitution), or otherwise embedding machine-generated content (JSON schema, mutation enums) into prompts that carry `.not.toContain(...)` guards.
**Anti-pattern:** Hardcoding the inlined text as a static snapshot — it silently drifts from the validator as the schema evolves. And leaving a negative guard keyed on a bare token such as `create_pull_request` / `submit_pull_request_review` — once the full schema enum is inlined, that token legitimately appears in the prompt, so the guard stops proving the *instruction* is absent (it false-passes, or fails spuriously).
**Example:** ENG-5320 — `renderAgentResultSchemaHelp(action)` in worker-result.ts feeds both `agent-result validate --help` and the prompt's `{{context:result-schema}}`; the guard asserts the rendered prompt `.toContain(JSON.stringify(z.toJSONSchema(workerResultSchema.safeExtend({ action }))))`, and `.not.toContain("create_pull_request")` was rewritten to `.not.toContain("Use `create_pull_request` whenever")`.

title: Wiring whose effect is one arg to a mocked collaborator needs a call-args assertion, mutation-verified
content:
**Rule:** When a feature's entire effect is a single argument passed to a collaborator that the test mocks, assert the mock's call args (`expect(fn).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({...}))`) and confirm the assertion is a genuine guard by mutation-testing it — drop the argument and watch the test fail.

**When to apply:** Writing or reviewing "wiring" changes where behavior hinges on one call argument to a function that is mocked in the test, and the helper that computes the argument already has its own unit tests.

**Anti-pattern:** Mocking the collaborator but only asserting the unit ran or its return value, never inspecting the call args. A later refactor that drops the argument passes every existing test — the resolver/helper unit tests cover the value in isolation, not the wiring — so the feature silently regresses to default behavior with no failing test.

**Example:** ENG-5319 (PR #86) — `createAgentRunner` was mocked in `attempt-executor.test.ts` but its args were un-inspected, so the single line `continuation: isContinuation` at `attempt-executor.ts:276` that makes continuation tuning take effect was untested. Fix added `expect(createAgentRunner).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({ continuation: true }))` for a resumed session and `false` for first-pass, verified as a real guard by mutation (dropping the arg failed both). Complements the continuation-knob architecture learning (swap at construction, keep the session key on base tuning).

title: Keep continuation prompt prefixes cache-stable; thread persisted state instead of re-discovering
content:
**Rule:** When authoring or editing a `*-continuation` worker prompt template, mirror the non-continuation template's context-block set and ordering exactly, and thread already-persisted bootstrap state (checkpoint repos) into the prompt rather than letting the agent re-discover it.
**When to apply:** Any change to `prompts/templates/*-continuation.md` or to `render-worker-prompt.ts` for actions that run repeatedly on one task target (review/reviewer loops).
**Anti-pattern:** Substituting only a subset of context blocks — or reordering them — in the continuation variant. The cache prefix diverges from the initial prompt, so every continuation cold-starts by re-discovering task/PR/repo state via `gh pr view` + Linear queries. The ENG-5265 investigation measured ~$10-13 of cache-write tax per cold review, ~75% of a $521 single-ticket review loop. Anthropic's 5-minute prompt-cache TTL (the 1-hour beta is unavailable on Foreman's auth) makes prefix stability the lever.
**Example:** ENG-5318 restored the 5 dropped `{{context:*}}` blocks in review/reviewer-continuation templates in non-continuation order and added a `prior-checkpoint` block read from `review_checkpoint`/`reviewer_checkpoint` (keyed by `task_target_id`) so the agent SHA-compares instead of re-fetching.

title: Test the structured contract a feature delivers, not just the adjacent narrative
content:
**Rule:** When a feature's purpose is to pass specific structured fields through to a rendered output (prompt JSON, payload, DTO), assert each key and its deterministic fixture value — not only the human-readable summary rendered beside it.
**When to apply:** Writing or reviewing tests for code that serializes a structured record into a prompt/payload whose downstream consumer depends on exact key names.
**Anti-pattern:** Asserting only the narrative field (e.g. `priorPassSummary`) while the keys that are the actual point of the block (`headSha`, `reviewThreadsFingerprint`, `checksFingerprint`, and the rest) go unasserted. Renaming or dropping a producer key keeps every test green while silently removing the data the consumer needs — the feature breaks but CI stays green.
**Example:** ENG-5318 review flagged this in `prompts.test.ts`; the fix (1ad4cfd) pinned `headSha`/`mergeState` values plus presence of the remaining fingerprint keys, and covered the two `resolvePriorPassSummary` fallbacks (`(prior pass recorded no summary)`, `(prior pass summary unavailable)`).

title: Foreman UI eslint is red on clean master — don't fix unrelated lint in scoped PRs
content:
**Rule:** Treat `yarn workspace @foreman/ui lint` as pre-existing-red: clean `master` reports ~15 problems (12 errors — `react-hooks/set-state-in-effect`, `react-refresh/only-export-components`, `no-control-regex`). In a scoped PR keep only your touched lines clean; do NOT fix the unrelated errors.
**When to apply:** Any foreman UI task whose acceptance criteria say "lint passes," or when `ui` lint fails right after your change.
**Anti-pattern:** Assuming your edit broke lint and reverting good work, or expanding scope to fix errors across untouched files (violates "no unrelated diff churn").
**How to confirm pre-existing:** `git stash && yarn workspace @foreman/ui lint && git stash pop` — same problem count on clean HEAD proves it.
**Example:** ENG-5373 (CSS + className-only) showed an identical 15-problem count with and without the change.

title: Verify foreman UI changes via Vite dev proxied to the running server (read-only)
content:
**Rule:** To visually verify a foreman UI change, run `yarn workspace @foreman/ui dev` (Vite :4173) and let its built-in `/api` proxy hit the already-running foreman server (default :8765) for real data, then drive agent-browser (`set viewport`, `open`, `eval`, `screenshot`). Stay READ-ONLY: only view pages — do not edit a Settings field, because Settings PATCHes the live workspace config on blur/click.
**When to apply:** Verifying layout/spacing/legibility fixes, or reproducing an audit-reported UI issue, before committing.
**Anti-pattern:** Booting the full orchestrator yourself (triggers live scouting/dispatch), or trusting an audit claim without reproducing it. ENG-5373's "mobile right gutter at 390px" did not reproduce (symmetric `px-4`, 29px input gutter, no overflow), so the right move was to measure, document, and skip the change rather than add churn.
**Example:** `agent-browser set viewport 390 844; open http://127.0.0.1:4173/settings; eval '<sub-11px audit snippet>'` returned 50 then 0 nodes after the token fix.

title: Gate query hooks inside Sheet/Dialog drawers with an `enabled` flag
content:
**Rule:** A detail drawer built on the `Sheet` primitive (Radix `Dialog.Root`) must gate every data-fetching hook in its body with an `enabled` flag tied to the open/selected state — never assume a closed drawer doesn't fetch.
**When to apply:** Adding or reviewing a Sheet/Dialog-based drawer whose body calls `useQuery`-style hooks, especially any with `refetchInterval` polling (e.g. `useAttemptsQuery`).
**Anti-pattern:** Relying on "the drawer is closed" to stop fetches. Radix `Dialog.Root` renders its children unconditionally, so the drawer component mounts on every render of the host page; with a null id the hook resolves to the *unfiltered* query and polls it (10s interval) for every user sitting on the page.
**Example:** ENG-5291 / PR #82 — `useAttemptsQuery({ taskId: taskId ?? undefined, enabled: taskId !== null })`, mirroring `AttemptDetailSheet`'s `enabled: Boolean(attemptId)` (fix in commit 1cdbe8a).

title: Don't derive canonical totals from a window-bound secondary source — show an affordance
content:
**Rule:** When a drawer/detail surface pairs a window-bound or filtered aggregate (e.g. a rollup bucket) with a non-window-bound detail query (e.g. the full attempts list), and the aggregate can be null for an entity excluded from the current window/filter, render an explicit "summary unavailable for the current window/filter" affordance instead of re-deriving the header totals from the detail query.
**When to apply:** A deep-linkable drawer whose header summary comes from a windowed/filtered rollup while its body list comes from an unfiltered query — so the two data sources can disagree on which entities exist.
**Anti-pattern:** Falling back to summing the loaded detail rows to fill the header. Those rows may be window-truncated, so the displayed total silently diverges from the canonical rollup figure while still looking authoritative.
**Example:** ENG-5291 / PR #82 — a deep link to `?taskId=` outside the 30-day rollup window left `selectedBucket` null; the fix (commit 1147043) shows a note that the rollup summary is unavailable and keeps the complete attempts list, rather than summing the loaded rows.

title: Name task-keyed attempt rollups Task*, not WorkItem* — "Work item" is the reserved task∪cron umbrella term
content:
**Rule:** When adding a new surface that aggregates execution attempts grouped by `taskId` (query carries `WHERE job.task_id IS NOT NULL`, so cron is excluded), name its types/files/repo-methods/endpoint/page `Task*` / `TaskRollup*` and label the UI "Tasks" — never coin a new `WorkItem*` vocabulary.
**When to apply:** Creating a rollup endpoint, UI page, repo method, or type in foreman that rolls execution attempts up per Linear task.
**Anti-pattern:** Coining `WorkItem*` for a task-only object. "Work item" is already the established umbrella term for *task ∪ cron* in the Attempts/Overview tables — the `title="Work item"` column plus `workItemLabel`/`workItemValue` helpers (`isCronJob ? "Cron job" : "Task"`) that render task-id OR cron-job-id. Reusing it for a cron-excluded, task-only rollup is actively misleading (a reader expects cron rollups too) and forces a full rename: ENG-5290 paid for two passes — `9cf6157` (data symbols) and `8249014` (page chrome) — before merge. Leave the existing "Work item" column untouched; task∪cron is correct there.
**Example:** `work-item-rollup.ts`→`task-rollup.ts`, `rollupWorkItems()`→`rollupTasks()`, `AttemptWorkItemRow`→`AttemptTaskRow`, `GET /api/work-items`→`GET /api/task-rollups` (NOT `/api/tasks` — collides with the existing task-mirror routes), nav label "Tasks".

title: Rollup endpoints must compute totals from the post-filter buckets they return
content:
**Rule:** When a rollup/aggregation endpoint applies `status`/`search` filters to its `buckets` and also returns aggregate `totals`, compute the totals from the same filtered bucket set the response emits — not from the pre-filter rollup.
**When to apply:** Building or extending a foreman aggregation endpoint that both filters buckets and returns summed totals (e.g. `/api/task-rollups`, `/api/usage`).
**Anti-pattern:** Returning `totals: rollup.totals` (computed before the bucket filter) next to filtered `buckets`. The two silently diverge and the API is internally inconsistent the moment any client renders totals — "the UI doesn't show totals yet" is not a reason to ship the inconsistency. Flagged in review and fixed in ENG-5290 `097168f`; the merged shape sums over `filteredBuckets` via `sumTaskRollupTotals(filteredBuckets)`.
**Example:** Build `filteredBuckets` first, then `totals: sumTaskRollupTotals(filteredBuckets)` so totals always equal the visible rows.

title: Docker runtime needs a devDependency: promote it in the deps stage, don't COPY the node_modules subtree
content:
**Rule:** When a warehousing-service Docker runtime process (e.g. `prisma db seed`) needs a package that lives in `devDependencies`, add the package name to the `runtimeDevDeps` array in `projects/warehousing-service/Dockerfile`'s `deps` stage — the `node -e` script that moves selected devDeps into `dependencies` before `yarn install --production`. Do not `COPY --from=build /app/node_modules/<pkg>` into the runtime stage.
**When to apply:** A Docker seed/migrate step fails with `Cannot find module '<dev-only-pkg>'`, or you otherwise need a dev-only package present in the Docker runtime image.
**Anti-pattern:** COPYing one resolved `node_modules/<pkg>` subtree forward carries only that directory; transitive deps hoisted elsewhere in `node_modules` are missed, so it breaks at runtime. Promotion lets `yarn install --production` resolve the package with its full dependency tree. The COPY approach was flagged and rejected in PR review.
**Example:** `seed.ts` imports `@invenco/test-utils/data/warehousing`; the fix added `'@invenco/test-utils'` to `runtimeDevDeps`, keeping the package dev-only everywhere else. (shipping-service instead bundles its seed via esbuild, so this promotion list is warehousing-specific.)

title: Don't let post-success cleanup validation roll back a completed attempt
content:
**Rule:** In attempt finalization, isolate best-effort cleanup (session deactivation, teardown) from the success path — wrap it in its own try/catch and downgrade failures to a warning so a validation error cannot escape to the outer catch and flip an already-completed/persisted attempt to `failed`.
**When to apply:** Any post-success branch in attempt-executor (or a similar finalizer) that calls a *validating* helper on data unrelated to the work that just succeeded — e.g. resolving a different role's runner config to deactivate its session after a completed retry.
**Anti-pattern:** `resolveRunnerConfigForAction(config, "reviewer", task)` during completed-retry cleanup throws `invalid_runner_override` when the task's reviewer tuning is invalid for the workspace reviewer provider; the throw hits the outer catch and marks an already-persisted retry attempt+job `failed`. Validate task overrides at the runner-construction boundary (where the offending runner actually runs), not opportunistically during finalization.
**Example:** ENG-5273 / PR #78 wrapped reviewer-session deactivation in try/catch + `attemptLogger.warn`, with a regression test asserting a completed retry stays `completed` when the reviewer override is invalid.

title: Model cross-provider runner tuning as a neutral domain field, map at the resolver
content:
**Rule:** When adding task/domain metadata that different runner providers interpret differently, store one provider-neutral field and map it to each provider's native field at the resolver boundary — keep provider-specific names out of the shared task domain.
**When to apply:** Extending Foreman runner overrides, or any task-domain type consumed by multiple runner providers (Claude/Codex use `effort`, OpenCode uses `variant`).
**Anti-pattern:** Putting `effort?` and `variant?` directly on the domain `TaskRunnerRoleOverride` leaks runner implementation detail into the task domain (flagged in review). Final shape used a single `tuning` field resolved per active provider, with the old names retained only as input aliases so existing/mirrored task files still hydrate.
**Example:** ENG-5273 / PR #78 commit "use provider-neutral runner override tuning".

title: Plumb optional Claude CLI flags through the 3-layer runner path
content:
**Rule:** To forward a new optional `claude` CLI flag through Foreman's Claude runner, thread it through exactly three layers and keep all provider-specific handling in the runner: (1) add the field to `claudeRunnerSchema` in `src/workspace/config.ts`, (2) accept it as an optional constructor arg and emit it in the argv builder in `src/execution/impl/claude-runner.ts`, (3) pass it from `config` in `src/execution/create-agent-runner.ts`.
**When to apply:** Implementing any ticket that plumbs a new optional `claude` runner flag/option (e.g. `--max-budget-usd`; the still-open sibling `--exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections` follows the same path).
**Anti-pattern:** Adding the schema field but forgetting the `create-agent-runner.ts` wiring — the value parses and validates but never reaches argv, so the flag silently never fires. Also avoid hand-concatenating the flag; reuse the conditional-spread idiom already established for `--resume`/`--session-id`: `...(x !== undefined ? ["--flag", String(x)] : [])`.
**Example:** `maxBudgetUsd: z.number().positive().optional()` -> `new ClaudeRunner(model, effort, config.maxBudgetUsd)` -> `...(this.maxBudgetUsd !== undefined ? ["--max-budget-usd", String(this.maxBudgetUsd)] : [])`. Tests: add argv presence/absence/fractional cases in `claude-runner.test.ts` via the shared `setUpFakeClaude()` helper (don't inline the `FOREMAN_CLAUDE_BIN` temp-script setup — concurrent flag-add PRs collide there, and PR #77's master-merge conflict was resolved by extracting that shared helper), plus a schema-validation case in `config.test.ts`.

title: Adding/reordering a runner CLI flag breaks exact-argv tests in two files
content:
**Rule:** When you add, remove, or reorder a CLI flag in a runner's `args` array (`src/execution/impl/<runner>-runner.ts`), update EVERY exact-argv assertion — both the runner's own `__tests__/<runner>-runner.test.ts` full-array `toEqual`, AND the shared `provider runners > selects the configured provider per action` test in `src/execution/__tests__/runner.test.ts`, which slices argv positionally (`argv.slice(0, N)`) and references the session id by numeric index (`argv[N]`).

**When to apply:** Editing any runner's spawned argv in Foreman.

**Anti-pattern:** Updating only the runner's dedicated test and running just that file. The positional-slice assertion lives in a SEPARATE file (`runner.test.ts`) and still encodes the old arg count/order, so it fails later in CI — and both the slice length AND the `nativeSessionId === argv[N]` index must shift, not just the length.

**Example:** ENG-5271 inserted `--exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections` after `--dangerously-skip-permissions`; the provider-runners test moved from `argv.slice(0, 10)` / `argv[9]` to `argv.slice(0, 11)` / `argv[10]`.

title: Attempt runnerVariant persists effort/variant, never "default"
content:
**Rule:** When keying logic off an attempt's `runnerVariant`, remember it persists `runnerTuningValue(runnerConfig)` — the configured effort for claude/codex (`high`, `max`, `xhigh`) or `variant` for opencode (`standard`, ...) — never the literal `"default"`.
**When to apply:** Building any lookup, filter, group-by, or session match keyed on `runnerVariant` (cost rate tables, usage rollups, runner-session selection).
**Anti-pattern:** Hardcoding `runnerVariant: "default"` in a lookup key. Every real attempt misses the exact-match and falls through to the zero/empty/unknown path silently — no throw, just wrong numbers.
**Example:** ENG-5270's rate table keyed `claude-opus-4-7|default` costed every default-config Opus attempt at $0 (the exact regression the ticket existed to prevent). Fix: drop `runnerVariant` from the key entirely since vendor pricing is model-level today; pricing only needs `runnerName|runnerModel`.

title: Don't duplicate a server-owned table on the client; hydrate it
content:
**Rule:** When the UI must compute a derived value (cost, totals) from a constants table the server already owns, expose the table via an endpoint and hydrate it client-side rather than hand-copying the table into UI code. Make the client a pure formatter/lookup over server-supplied data so both surfaces compute from one source.
**When to apply:** Any full-stack derived-value surface where a shared constant table drives both a server rollup and a client-side per-row computation.
**Anti-pattern:** A hand-synced duplicate table (even one commented "kept in sync by hand"). A one-file rate bump silently diverges the client column from the server rollup, and the disagreement is invisible to anyone reading either total.
**Example:** ENG-5270 first shipped `ui/src/lib/cost.ts` duplicating `src/execution/cost/rates.ts`; review required adding `GET /api/rates` + a `useRatesQuery` hook so the UI hydrates the rate table and `estimateCost` looks it up against server data.

title: Cross-repo enums coupled by dynamic key lookup must change in lockstep
content:
**Rule:** When two enums defined in different packages are bridged by a dynamic key lookup (e.g. `ShippingMethodRateMatchMethod[carrierService.rateMatchMethod]`), their member names/values must stay byte-identical; rename or extend BOTH sides in one coordinated change, then republish the upstream package and bump the downstream consumer.
**When to apply:** Adding to, renaming, or reviewing a member of `@invenco/common-domain` `ShippingMethodRateMatchMethod` or shipping-service `CarrierServiceRateMatchMethod` — or any pair of enums where one is indexed by the other's value.
**Anti-pattern:** Renaming one enum's members (e.g. POSTCODE -> TO_POSTCODE) without the other. TypeScript does NOT error across separately-defined enums, so the lookup silently returns `undefined` at runtime — here that means LOCALITY carrier services would get no default rate-match-method.
**Example:** ENG-5267 renamed common-domain to TO_POSTCODE/TO_LOCALITY (published 4.11.0); the coupling held only because ENG-5265 (shipping PR #575) matched the exact names on CarrierServiceRateMatchMethod and bumped the dep. The agent explicitly flagged this constraint on the downstream ticket.

title: Ticket 'locked decisions' yield to maintainer override in PR review; propagate the change downstream
content:
**Rule:** A ticket's "Decisions locked" / in-scope section is a planning-time default, not immutable. If a maintainer overrides it during PR review, comply — and then explicitly propagate the consequence to any dependent/downstream ticket rather than assuming the other slice will notice.
**When to apply:** A reviewer requests a change that contradicts something the ticket marked as locked/decided (naming, shape, scope), especially when the change has cross-slice implications.
**Anti-pattern:** Over-anchoring on the "locked" label and refusing the change, or complying silently without flagging dependent work — leaving a sibling ticket to ship the now-stale decision.
**Example:** ENG-5267 locked the enum names as POSTCODE/LOCALITY; the reviewer forced TO_POSTCODE/TO_LOCALITY. The agent pushed back once with the coupling rationale, then complied and flagged the matching-names requirement on downstream ENG-5265, which landed correctly.

title: Cache write path must reuse the read path's lookup query so identity can't drift
content:
**Rule:** When a read-through cache's read path matches rows by a domain-configured grain (e.g. a set of rateMatchMethod-driven OR branches), the cache write/upsert path must reuse that exact same lookup query — never a parallel "request signature" path — so write-grain and read-grain cannot diverge.
**When to apply:** Adding or changing how cached rows are deduped/matched on write in CarrierRateService, or any read-through cache where reads filter by a configurable key-grain.
**Anti-pattern:** A dedicated `CarrierRateRequestSignature` + `getByRequestSignature` write path with its own where-clause. It silently drifts from the read's grain logic and fans the cache out below read grain (the original TGE unusable-cache bug). ENG-5265 deleted that whole path during review and routed `cacheGatewayRates` through the same `getByCarrierServices` query the read uses.
**Example:** Normalise grain once at the write boundary in the app layer (`alignRateToGrain` nulls to-side fields below the carrier's grain), then look up with `getByCarrierServices`; the repo only maps/persists.

title: Extending a RateMatchMethod grain enum spans both rate repos + common-domain — extend together or reads silently return empty
content:
**Rule:** A new `RateMatchMethod`/cache-grain enum value must be added in lockstep across all three surfaces, using identical names: (a) `CarrierServiceRateMatchMethod` (Prisma + TS) and `PrismaCarrierRateRepo` read branches, (b) `ShippingMethodRateMatchMethod` (Prisma) and `PrismaShippingRateRepo` read branches, and (c) the cross-repo `@invenco/common-domain` `ShippingMethodRateMatchMethod` (publish that first, then bump the dep).
**When to apply:** Adding or renaming any rateMatchMethod / cache-grain enum value in shipping-service.
**Anti-pattern:** Touching only CarrierRate (as the plan originally scoped). A ShippingMethod defaulted from a carrier service with the new value (`CreateShippingMethod.ts`: `ShippingMethodRateMatchMethod[carrierService.rateMatchMethod]`) resolves to `undefined`, and its ShippingRates silently miss on read. The reviewer required the ShippingRate repo + enum mirror the plan omitted.
**Example:** ENG-5265 added `TO_POSTCODE`/`TO_LOCALITY` to both repos and shipped the same names in common-domain 4.11.0 before bumping `^4.7.0`→`^4.11.0`. Also note: a plan's "locked" name (`LOCALITY`) can still be changed in review to match an existing in-repo convention (`TO_LOCALITY` ↔ `TO_POSTCODE_RANGE`) — settle the final cross-repo name before the upstream package publishes.

title: `utils.ts` is a standing review-blocker — name files by purpose
content:
**Rule:** Do not introduce or grow a `utils.ts`; name the file after what it does and export a verb-named helper. A senior reviewer (iknowcss) consistently blocks `utils.ts` as a dumping-ground across PRs ("I keep banging on this drum but people keep ignoring it").
**When to apply:** Any time you reach for `utils.ts`/`helpers.ts` to home a new function, or when extracting shared logic during review.
**Anti-pattern:** Dropping grain/cache logic into `gateways/carrierGateway/impl/utils.ts`. In ENG-5265 the helper stayed a purpose-named static, and a follow-up refactor split an existing `impl/utils.ts` into a purpose-named module rather than adding to it.
**Example:** Prefer `cacheGrain.ts` exporting `stripToCacheGrain` over a generic `utils.ts`.

title: Truthiness-gating an optional query field ('' is falsy) disables the branch and can mask tests
content:
**Rule:** When a repo where-clause branch is gated on the truthiness of an optional field (e.g. `if (query.from.city)`), remember `''` is falsy — an empty string disables the branch exactly like `undefined`. Gate on explicit presence if `''` must be distinguished, and confirm each test still exercises the branch it claims to.
**When to apply:** Adding optional-field-gated OR branches in `buildFromClause`/`buildToClause` and writing/reviewing their integration tests.
**Anti-pattern:** A test that set `from.city = ''` skipped the `TO_LOCALITY` branch, returned `[]`, and was the root cause of the "Test apps" CI failure; a sibling "suburb missing" test still passed only because BOTH gated branches were disabled — it had silently stopped asserting the `to.city` gate it was named for. A green test can stop testing what it claims once a gated branch is off.
**Example:** Fixed by setting `from.city: 'Sydney'` to match the mock's `fromAddress.city`.

title: Verify 'what landed' against GitHub, not the worktree's stale local origin/master
content:
**Rule:** During consolidation/review, determine "what actually landed" from GitHub (`gh pr diff <n>`, the merge commit via the API), not from the worktree's local `origin/<base>` ref — long-lived Foreman worktrees are often not re-fetched, so their `origin/master` can predate a recently-merged PR.
**When to apply:** Any consolidation or review pass that inspects merged code, asserts a final design, or greps for added/removed symbols.
**Anti-pattern:** `git grep origin/master` in the worktree showed the pre-PR design (`getByRequestSignature` present, no `alignRateToGrain`/`TO_LOCALITY`) even though PR #575 had merged hours earlier — trusting it would have produced a ticket comment and learnings describing a design that was scrapped in review.
**Example:** Cross-check with `gh pr diff 575` (authoritative) before writing any "final landed design" note.

title: Trace cross-service consumers before turning a 2xx into a 4xx
content:
**Rule:** Before changing an endpoint's success (2xx) response into an error (4xx) for some inputs, trace every cross-service consumer and confirm its error handling already degrades gracefully under the new status.
**When to apply:** Any change that converts a previously-OK response into a 4xx/5xx on an endpoint consumed by other Lynk services (e.g. through a generated `Http*Gateway`).
**Anti-pattern:** Assuming a stricter status is "obviously safe." Lynk `Http*Gateway` clients (got-based) rethrow on non-2xx, so a brand-new 4xx surfaces as a *thrown* error inside every consumer's gateway call — silently changing control flow in services you never edited.
**Example:** ENG-5252 made POST /quotes return 422 when a filter matched 0 ShippingMethods. A reviewer flagged possible sales-service breakage; tracing `HttpShippingGateway.createQuote` -> `FulfillmentService.applyDynamicShippingProfile` showed the call is already wrapped in try/catch that applies SHIPPING_HOLD/NO_PREFERRED_RATE, so the order-state outcome was unchanged and only the previously-misleading warn log improved — which unblocked approval.

title: Empty filtered result: distinguish misconfiguration from legitimately empty
content:
**Rule:** When a caller-supplied filter resolves to zero rows, branch on whether the filter was present: filter supplied + 0 results = invalid scope -> return a typed sad error surfaced as 4xx; no filter + 0 results = legitimate empty -> return a happy empty result (2xx).
**When to apply:** Any "no results" path where the query is narrowed by caller-provided IDs/filters (e.g. shipping-service `getShippingMethodsForQuoteRequest` keyed on `carrierServiceIds`/`shippingMethodIds`).
**Anti-pattern:** Collapsing both cases into one empty-success response — a stale/typo'd filter ID then becomes indistinguishable from "no rates today." That was the ENG-5252 silent failure: 201 with empty rates shipped to stage and cost ~an hour of log archaeology before the bogus carrier-service ID was found.
**Example:** `getShippingMethodsForQuoteRequest` now returns `NoMatchingShippingMethodsError` (-> 422, message names the offending filter type/IDs/account/location) when a filter is supplied and the result is empty, while still returning a happy empty `ShippingMethod[]` (201) when no filter is supplied.

title: Wire new persisted fields through domain/mapper in the column-add PR, not as a dark column
content:
**Rule:** When adding a new persisted column to a Lynk entity, wire it through the domain entity + Prisma mapper in the same PR that adds the column; reserve a nullable "dark column" for fields that genuinely cannot be modeled yet.
**When to apply:** A ticket prescribes a multi-stage "add column now (dark), wire the domain in a later PR" plan for a Lynk service.
**Anti-pattern:** Shipping a nullable dark column + bespoke SQL backfill.
