In ENG-5159 a maintainer (@ashishtilara) rejected exactly this and asked to hook the field through domain/mapper so the follow-up is a pure usage swap; the merged design became `ADD COLUMN … JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]'` with `bracketsConfig: BracketConfigEntry[]` validated in the entity, populated as `[]` everywhere, real data deferred to a data-only follow-up — dropping the SQL backfill and its 334-line integration test entirely.
**Example:** PR #559 — the ticket's explicit "Out of scope: no domain entity changes" was overridden by reviewer direction and the column shipped NOT NULL DEFAULT instead of nullable + backfill.

title: Consolidate from the merged commit, not the local worktree
content:
**Rule:** During consolidation or post-merge review of a merged PR, derive the landed outcome by diffing the merged commit (`git show <mergedSha>` / `gh pr diff`), never by reading the local worktree HEAD.
**When to apply:** Any consolidation where the worktree branch and the PR head SHA differ (`git merge-base --is-ancestor <prHead> HEAD` returns false).
**Anti-pattern:** Trusting the worktree. In ENG-5159 the worktree sat at the pre-pivot design (nullable column + SQL backfill + `buildBracketsConfig` helper) while the PR was squash-rewritten to a different design (`NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]'` + domain wiring, backfill test deleted). A worktree-only read reports the wrong outcome and misses the entire review-driven pivot.
**Example:** worktree `5ac8f88f` vs merged `881101e9` — different files, different migration, opposite domain-wiring decision.

title: Two paths emitting the same serialized shape need a parity test on edge fixtures — or collapse to one source
content:
**Rule:** When two derivations must emit byte-identical serialized output (e.g. a TS seed/mapper and a SQL migration backfill both building the same JSONB), either parity-test them on adversarial fixtures (decimal/non-integer, empty, boundary) or eliminate the duplication so there is a single source of truth.
**When to apply:** A migration/backfill computes a JSON shape that application code (seed, mapper) also computes, or any time the same persisted value is produced in more than one place.
**Anti-pattern:** Testing only integer/happy fixtures, where SQL `::numeric` and JS IEEE-754 `number` coincide and hide drift on fractional values; the reviewer flagged this as the critical issue in ENG-5159 (fixture `[0.3, 0.5, 1, 2.5]` × `250.5`).
**Example:** the merged PR sidestepped the hazard entirely by collapsing to one source — a DB `DEFAULT '[]'` plus a single validated domain shape — so there was no second derivation to drift.

title: Add new seed rows inline beside their siblings, not via a new domain/ abstraction
content:
**Rule:** When adding the Nth entry to a homogeneous collection in a seed file (carrier/service/account rows in `prisma/idempotent-seed.ts`), declare it inline exactly like the existing N-1 siblings — pure-data bracket/config arrays as local consts plus rows in the existing config list. Do not introduce a new `domain/` file, a builder function, or a dedicated unit test for what is constant data.
**When to apply:** Editing `seedCarrierWeightBrackets` / `seedWeightBracketsForCarrier` (or any sibling-heavy seed/fixture list) to add one more carrier, service, or account — especially when tempted by a "unit-testable in isolation" rationale.
**Anti-pattern:** ENG-5137 (PR #558) first added `domain/tgeWeightBrackets.ts` + a `buildTgeBracketSeedRows` builder + a test for ~30 lines of constant data. Both the maintainer ("this should live within the seed step only … seed rows are not domain logic") and the review agent flagged the pattern divergence and the position-coupled `[0]/[1]/[2]/[3]` test assertions. Inlining the consts next to `AUSPOST_*_BRACKETS` and the rows into `bracketConfigs` (deleting the file + test, -131 lines) got it approved. The ticket had already specified the inline approach; the over-abstraction was a self-inflicted deviation. Reinforces the repo guardrail "follow the established create/update patterns before introducing a custom save path".
**Example:** TGE → `TGE_PARCEL_B2C_BRACKETS` / `TGE_ROAD_EXPRESS_BRACKETS` consts + 4 rows in `bracketConfigs`, mirroring the 5 existing carriers.

title: Consolidating a stacked PR: confirm it reached master, don't trust the MERGED badge
content:
**Rule:** When consolidating or verifying a PR whose base branch is a dependency branch (a stacked PR, base != master), read the relevant file on `origin/master` to confirm the change's intent is actually present there — do not treat the PR's `MERGED` state as proof the code shipped to trunk.
**When to apply:** Any consolidation of a Foreman dependent task, or any time `gh pr view` shows `baseRefName` != the trunk branch.
**Anti-pattern:** Assuming merged-into-base implies on-master. The base branch can be refactored, squashed, or rewritten before it merges upward, so the exact symbols a PR added may be absent from master even though the work "landed". In ENG-5137, PR #558 merged into `eng-5136`; by the time `eng-5136` reached master the whole `seedCarrierWeightBrackets` function had been replaced by a data-driven `seedWeightBracketsForCarrier`, so the merged PR's symbols (`seedCarrierWeightBrackets`, `TGE_PARCEL_B2C_BRACKETS`) are gone from master while the intent (TGE bracket seeding, `idempotent-seed.ts:809`) persists.
**Example:** `git merge-base --is-ancestor <pr-merge-sha> origin/master` returned false (base merged via a true/squash merge, not fast-forward); reading the file on master then confirmed the intent survived via the refactor.

title: Scout-run per-run metrics must be recorded at completeScoutRun, not createScoutRun
content:
**Rule:** To persist a per-scout-run metric, return it from `runScoutSelection` and merge it into the scheduler's `completeScoutRun` summary (or add a dedicated `scout_run` column) — never via `createScoutRun`'s `summary`.
**When to apply:** Adding any new observable count/metric to a scout run (skipped/excluded/filtered counts, capacity stats, etc.).
**Anti-pattern:** Passing `summary: {...}` to `createScoutRun`. `SchedulerService` finishes every run with `completeScoutRun({ summary: { enqueued } })`, which overwrites `summary_json` wholesale, so an intake-time summary never survives to the persisted row. Only the durable count columns (candidate/active/terminal) set at `createScoutRun` persist from creation.
**Example:** ENG-5384 returns `excludedByLabelCount` from `runScoutSelection` and folds it into `completeScoutRun`'s summary.

title: Scout-run durable summary is written at completeScoutRun, not createScoutRun
content:
**Rule:** To persist or test a field on a Foreman scout-run summary, write/assert it at the `completeScoutRun` call in `scheduler-service.ts`, not at `createScoutRun` in `scout-selection.ts`.
**When to apply:** Adding any durable field to the scout-run record (e.g. excludedByLabelCount, enqueued) or testing that it persists.
**Anti-pattern:** `createScoutRun` sets initial counts but its summary is overwritten by `completeScoutRun`, so a value placed only at creation is lost; and unit-testing only `runScoutSelection`'s return value leaves the `completeScoutRun({ summary })` wiring uncovered, so an inverted guard or dropped field persists a silently-wrong record with no failing test.
**Example:** ENG-5384 records `excludedByLabelCount` via `...(count > 0 ? { excludedByLabelCount } : {})` in the `completeScoutRun` summary, but coverage existed only on the scout-selection return value.

title: Test scout-run metric persistence at the scheduler layer, not just runScoutSelection's return
content:
**Rule:** When a scout metric is returned by `runScoutSelection` and folded into the scheduler's `completeScoutRun` summary behind a conditional (e.g. `> 0`), add a scheduler-level test that drives `(scheduler as any).runScout(...)` and asserts the exact `completeScoutRun` summary — asserting only `runScoutSelection`'s return leaves the guard and the persistence call uncovered.
**When to apply:** Adding or changing any per-run count/metric that `SchedulerService.runScout` writes onto the scout-run summary.
**Anti-pattern:** Covering only the producer's return value; an inverted guard (`> 0` -> `=== 0`) or a dropped return field then persists a silently-wrong summary with no test failing.
**Example:** ENG-5384 — `createMockRepos` + `runScout('poll')` with one excluded-label `done` candidate asserts `summary: { enqueued: 0, excludedByLabelCount: 1 }`; an already-consolidated no-exclusion run asserts the key is absent.

title: parseLinearMetadata needs a non-empty default branch to emit targets
content:
**Rule:** When reusing `parseLinearMetadata(description, defaultBranchName)` to derive targets/frontmatter outside the Linear task system, pass a non-empty `defaultBranchName` (e.g. `task.id.toLowerCase()`), mirroring `linearIssueToTask`'s `node.branchName ?? node.identifier.toLowerCase()` fallback.
**When to apply:** Deriving repos/targets from a Linear issue description anywhere other than `linearIssueToTask` — e.g. HTTP `GET /api/tasks` frontmatter, the issue-manager UI, at-risk detection.
**Anti-pattern:** Passing `undefined`/`""`. `parseLinearMetadata` only builds `targets` when an effective branch name resolves (an explicit `Branch:` line OR the default), so a valid `Agent:` + `Repos:` block yields zero targets and gets misclassified as broken/no-repos.
**Example:** ENG-5385's `computeTaskFrontmatter` passes `task.id.toLowerCase()`; with `undefined` the frontmatter would report `broken` for a description that has a usable `Repos:` but no explicit `Branch:` line.

title: Foreman full yarn test has environmental 5s-timeout flakes in subprocess suites
content:
**Rule:** Treat `yarn test` failures that are confined to foreman's git/process-spawning suites — `git-worktrees`, `runner`, `foreman-version`, `scheduler` reviewer-session — as environmental 5000ms-timeout contention, not regressions. Confirm by checking the failing suites don't import your changed modules and re-running them in isolation (failure count drops as parallelism drops).
**When to apply:** A foreman ticket's verify step runs the full `yarn test` and reports a handful of failures while your change only touches unrelated modules (e.g. http / tasking).
**Anti-pattern:** Concluding your change broke something, or trying to "fix" those suites — they time out under parallel load regardless of the diff, and chasing them wastes the run.
**Example:** ENG-5385 (touching only `http.ts` + `linear-task-system.ts`) saw 8 such failures in the full run; all passed when each suite ran in isolation, and none reference the changed files.

title: Check upstream gating before flagging a serialize-time parser throw as a list regression
content:
**Rule:** Before flagging a newly-introduced throwing parser at serialize-time as a list-breaking regression, verify a throwing input can actually reach it — trace whether ingestion (mirror save) or live-fetch already runs the same parser and would have rejected the value first.
**When to apply:** Reviewing a change that calls a throwing parser (e.g. parseLinearMetadata) inside per-item serialization of a list endpoint (GET /api/tasks via Promise.all).
**Anti-pattern:** Raising "one malformed item fails the whole list" without checking that malformed items are gated upstream; every task in the mirror already passed linearIssueToTask's parse, so re-parsing the same description can't be the first throw.
**Example:** computeTaskFrontmatter re-runs parseLinearMetadata on task.description, but a `Depends on branches:` block would have thrown at linearIssueToTask ingestion before the task was ever mirrored — so the throw is unreachable from the list endpoint.

title: Foreman UI lint is red on the base branch; validate only your own files
content:
**Rule:** When a foreman ticket requires `yarn workspace @foreman/ui lint` to pass, validate that the files you added/changed are lint-clean; do not try to drive the whole workspace to zero problems.
**When to apply:** Any foreman UI task whose acceptance criteria include lint passing.
**Anti-pattern:** Treating the ~12 pre-existing eslint errors (introduced by the ENG-5381 dependency upgrade — stricter react-hooks/react-refresh rules across vendored `components/ui`, `settings`, `data-table`, `attempts`, `overview`) as yours, then editing vendored `components/ui` (forbidden) or unrelated pages (unrelated diff churn) to silence them.
**Example:** base `eng-5385` lints at 15 problems (12 errors); after adding the Foreman page the count stays 15 — net-zero from the new files, even though the command still exits 1.

title: Split hook-using table cells into a *-cells.tsx to satisfy react-refresh
content:
**Rule:** When a new foreman UI table needs cell components that call hooks (e.g. a mutation for an inline control), put those components in a sibling `*-cells.tsx` that exports only components, and keep `columns.tsx` exporting just the column factory + filter fns.
**When to apply:** Adding a `pages/<x>/columns.tsx` whose cells need React hooks (mutations/queries) rather than the pure inline-arrow cells the tasks page uses.
**Anti-pattern:** Declaring `function FooCell()` components inside `columns.tsx` alongside non-component exports trips eslint `react-refresh/only-export-components` ("file only exports components"), failing lint on your own file.
**Example:** `FrontmatterCell`/`ForemanToggleCell` live in `pages/foreman/foreman-cells.tsx`; `columns.tsx` then exports only `createForemanColumns` + `foremanGlobalFilter`.

title: Reviewing Foreman UI: verify terse/keyed badge copy against the ticket spec before flagging it as a typo
content:
**Rule:** Before flagging Foreman UI label/badge copy that looks malformed (trailing colon, terse phrasing) as a display artifact, check the ticket's explicit design spec and the backend's `detail`/string conventions — such copy is frequently deliberate and references a literal metadata key.
**When to apply:** Reviewing a Foreman UI PR that renders status/frontmatter badges or other short labels.
**Anti-pattern:** Flagging the broken-frontmatter badge `"⚠ no Repos:"` as a dangling trailing colon. It is spec-mandated and references the literal `Repos:` key in the Agent metadata block; the backend emits the same form (`"Agent: block found, but no usable Repos:"`). A review agent flagged this at 82 confidence in ENG-5386 — a false positive.
**Example:** ENG-5386 frontmatter badges in `ui/src/pages/foreman/foreman-cells.tsx`.

title: Foreman scout mirrors only includeLabel-tagged issues — UI 'ready-but-untagged' scope branches are forward-compat headroom
content:
**Rule:** Before treating a Foreman UI 'ready-but-untagged' union/scope branch (or a `+ mark for Foreman`-style control gated on `!isAgentTagged`) as a live bug, confirm the scout still only mirrors includeLabel-tagged issues — the Linear candidate query filters `labels:{some:{name:{in:includeLabels}}}` (`src/tasking/impl/linear-task-system.ts`). While that holds, untagged rows can't reach `GET /api/tasks`, so such branches are unreachable forward-compat code, not reachable behavior.
**When to apply:** Reviewing Foreman UI that derives/filters over `GET /api/tasks` with a ready-state OR agent-tagged union, or controls gated on whether a row is agent-tagged.
**Anti-pattern:** Flagging the union's ready clause, or the enable-mutation reuse behind `+ mark for Foreman` (which removes exclude labels rather than adding an include label), as a live semantic bug. Today it cannot render, and ENG-5386 documents it as out-of-scope headroom.
**Example:** ENG-5386 `isInForemanScope` / `ForemanToggleCell` in `ui/src/pages/foreman/`.

title: Reviewer continuation: distinguish rebase-only head moves from new work via gh pr diff
content:
**Rule:** In a reviewer continuation pass where the PR head SHA changed, compare the current `gh pr diff <n>` (vs base) against the prior pass's surface — not `git diff <oldHead>..<newHead>`. If the PR-vs-base diff is unchanged, the head moved due to a rebase/force-push onto newer master, so there is no new PR code to review; return no_action_needed.
**When to apply:** Continuation reviewer pass where previousSessionHeadSha differs from the current head and the branch may have been rebased.
**Anti-pattern:** Diffing `oldHead..newHead` directly — after a rebase this is polluted with every unrelated ticket that landed on master (dep bumps, other features), tricking you into reviewing code that isn't part of this PR.
**Example:** ENG-5370 head went b16eabb→c6f0af9; `git diff` showed 10 files/400+ lines from ENG-5371/5372/5373/5381, but `gh pr diff` was the same 4-file scroll-shadow change — a clean rebase, corroborated by the author's force-push comment.

title: Reviewer text-less screenshots are verification, not a change request
content:
**Rule:** Treat a reviewer's text-less agent-browser screenshot as verification evidence, not an implicit change request — before touching code, confirm whether a failure it shows (e.g. an error toast) is actually the spec'd behavior.
**When to apply:** A continuation review pass where the only new activity is screenshot(s) with no accompanying text.
**Anti-pattern:** Inferring a bug from a failure screenshot and making a speculative UI change. On ENG-5386 the Foreman toggle's "requires taskSystem.linear.excludeLabels to be configured" toast is the backend's documented exclude_labels_not_configured 400 (ENG-5385) surfaced as the required toast-on-failure — it only fires when the workspace has no excludeLabels set, so it is expected, not a regression.
**Example:** PR #101 screenshots showed the manager rendering correctly plus that expected toast; the right move was a clarifying reply (and offering an optional disable-when-unconfigured guard), not a code change.

title: Foreman http tests mock deps.taskSystem, so provider query assembly needs task-system-<provider> tests
content:
**Rule:** When a change adds or alters a provider query (e.g. a Linear GraphQL query in `linear-task-system.ts`), require coverage in `src/tasking/impl/__tests__/task-system-<provider>.test.ts`; do not treat `src/__tests__/http.test.ts` as covering it.
**When to apply:** Reviewing or implementing any change to a `TaskSystem` provider method's query construction (`listCandidates`, `listAssignedIssues`, etc.).
**Anti-pattern:** Assuming an http integration test (e.g. the `scope=assigned` test) exercises the real query — http.test.ts injects `taskSystem` as `vi.fn()` mocks, so the real GraphQL assembly (variable declarations, filter clauses, dangling commas) never runs and a malformed query only fails at runtime against the provider.
**Example:** ENG-5386 ce924dd added `LinearTaskSystem.listAssignedIssues` (the no-label `queryAssignedIssues` branch) covered only by an http mock; the Linear-level assembly stayed untested.

title: Merging a live superset with a cached set: build the dedup key set from the full cache, not a limit/search-windowed query
content:
**Rule:** When a 'show everything' view unions a live/full provider set with a persisted/cached set, build the dedup key set from the entire cache (e.g. all mirrored rows / `tasksById`), not from a limit/search/state-windowed query result.
**When to apply:** Reviewing or writing an endpoint that merges a live provider query with a mirrored/cached set behind a completeness-contract view (e.g. Foreman's `GET /api/tasks?scope=assigned` 'All my tickets').
**Anti-pattern:** Deriving the dedup set from the windowed query (`candidateTasks` after `limit`/`search`) — a cached row outside that window is then re-served from the live set (losing persisted targets/jobs/reviews) or dropped entirely. Treat this as a real finding even when the window isn't hit at current scale: the view's contract is completeness, so silent row loss is a correctness bug, not a dormant edge to suppress.
**Example:** ENG-5386 `src/http.ts` — the maintainer flagged exactly this limit-window dedup (which an earlier reviewer pass had dropped as 'dormant'); the fix switched `mirroredIds` from `candidateTasks` to `tasksById.keys()`.

title: On shared/stacked Foreman PR branches, re-check remote before resolving conflicts; never force-push a redundant resolution
content:
**Rule:** On a PR branch shared by concurrent Foreman workers (stacked tickets on one branch, e.g. ENG-5384/5385/5386 all on eng-5384), re-fetch and re-check `gh pr view <n> --json mergeable` right before resolving a merge conflict and again before pushing. If `git push` is rejected as non-fast-forward and the PR already reports MERGEABLE/CLEAN, a concurrent worker has already resolved it — realign with `git reset --hard origin/<branch>` (stay on the branch, no detached HEAD) and stand down.
**When to apply:** A review/retry pass finds a merge conflict on a branch other workers also push to, or a push is rejected non-fast-forward.
**Anti-pattern:** Force-pushing your local resolution to 'win'. It clobbers a concurrent (often identical) resolution plus any rebased history, and can silently revert other tickets' work on the shared branch.
**Example:** ENG-5384 — resolved the src/http.ts conflict locally (drop inline resolveArtifactContentPath, keep ENG-5385/5386 helpers, use the extracted lib import); meanwhile origin/eng-5384 was force-pushed to an equivalent resolution. Push rejected, PR already MERGEABLE, so reset to origin and returned no_action_needed.

title: Reviewer continuation: verify new commits belong to the PR's ticket — rebases can stack sibling-ticket work
content:
**Rule:** On a reviewer continuation pass where the PR head jumped by multiple commits, verify each new commit actually belongs to the PR's ticket before reviewing the delta as in-scope.
**When to apply:** The `previousSessionHeadSha` -> `currentPrHeadSha` gap is larger than the expected follow-up commit(s), or the head changed shape (rebase / force-push).
**Anti-pattern:** A branch gets rebased onto master and sibling-ticket commits are stacked on top, silently bundling 2-3 tickets into one PR. A raw `git diff prevHead..newHead` also balloons with pulled-in master changes and hides this, so reviewing that delta as in-scope blesses the contamination.
**How to check:** `gh pr view <n> --json commits` (scan subjects for foreign ticket prefixes), `gh pr diff <n> --name-only` (PR-vs-base file scope vs the ticket's stated scope), and `gh pr list` (do the sibling tickets have their own PRs?). Confirm the in-scope work survived the rebase by diffing the old reviewed tip against the rebased same-ticket tip for just those files — empty = preserved.
**Example:** ENG-5384 PR #99 head moved 35f3f2e -> ccfcaea; the 5-commit jump was ENG-5385 (toggle API) + ENG-5386 (UI) stacked on a rebase with no separate PRs, bundling three tickets under the ENG-5384 PR.

title: On Foreman shared/stacked PR branches, escalate scope/branch concerns — never rewrite history from a single task worker
content:
**Rule:** When a PR's branch is shared by stacked tickets (multiple ENG-* on one branch, each with its own concurrent worker) and a review flags scope bleed — e.g. a PR tracked as ENG-A now also carries ENG-B/ENG-C commits with no separate PRs — respond by escalating: reply to the review thread (leave it unresolved) and add a task comment for the operator. Do NOT execute the remediation yourself.
**When to apply:** A current-head review thread/summary on a shared branch asks you to rebase-and-split, retitle/retrack, or otherwise restructure which tickets a PR carries; or a merge conflict on such a branch was already resolved by another worker.
**Anti-pattern:** Rebasing/force-pushing the shared branch to drop other tickets' commits (destroys concurrent in-flight work), or unilaterally retitling/rebundling the PR. Branch/PR topology is an orchestration decision, and there is no worker mutation to retitle a PR anyway.
**Example:** ENG-5384 PR #99 was rebased to stack ENG-5385 (toggle API) + ENG-5386 (UI) on top of the two ENG-5384 commits; reviewer asked to split or retrack. Replied on the thread + left a Linear task note for the operator; made no code/branch change.

title: Don't treat your own agent reply (empty-body current-head review) as new reviewer feedback
content:
**Rule:** Foreman posts all GitHub bot activity under one identity (e.g. `0x1NotMe`), separating roles only by body prefix — `[review agent]` for the reviewer, `[tars]` (the workspace agentPrefix) for the implementing worker. A worker's own `reply_to_thread_comment` lands as a NEW current-head PullRequestReview with an EMPTY summary body. On a continuation pass treat an empty-body current-head review, and any `[tars]`/agentPrefix thread or PR comment, as your OWN prior output — not actionable feedback.
**When to apply:** A continuation review/retry pass where the head is unchanged and the only 'new' review/thread activity is empty-bodied or carries the workspace agentPrefix.
**Anti-pattern:** Re-replying to or re-addressing your own escalation/answer — it loops the review indefinitely and spams the thread. Only act on `[review agent]` or human/maintainer content that post-dates your last reply; leave an already-escalated thread unresolved while awaiting the operator.
**Example:** ENG-5384 PR #99 — after escalating a scope-bleed thread, the next pass saw a new current-head review (PRR…CrtiyQ) with bodyLen 0 at the same timestamp as the `[tars]` reply; recognized it as self-output and returned no_action_needed.

title: Avoid `prisma format` for targeted schema.prisma edits — it reformats the whole file
content:
**Rule:** Make targeted manual edits to `schema.prisma`; do not run `prisma format` to tidy up after a small change.
**When to apply:** Any task editing `projects/shipping-service/prisma/schema.prisma` (removing `@default`, adding a column, etc.) where 'no unrelated diff churn' is a review/acceptance criterion.
**Anti-pattern:** `prisma format` reformats the ENTIRE file and silently re-aligns other already-committed-but-misaligned models (in ENG-5445 it re-aligned the whole `Surcharge` block), producing churn unrelated to the task. CI here does not enforce format, so the existing misalignment is the repo's canonical state.
**Example:** Removing a trailing `@default(...)` does not change the field-name/type column widths, so a plain Edit preserves alignment — no formatter needed.

title: Run Prisma via the repo-pinned binary, not global `npx prisma`
content:
**Rule:** Invoke Prisma through `node_modules/.bin/prisma` (after `yarn install`) or via the nx target (`yarn nx run shipping-service:typecheck` runs `prisma-generate` as a dependency), never bare `npx prisma`.
**When to apply:** Generating the client, validating, or formatting the schema in shipping-service (pinned to Prisma v6).
**Anti-pattern:** Bare `npx prisma` resolves to the latest major (v7.x), which rejects this repo's v6 schema with confusing errors (`datasource property url is no longer supported`, `preview feature "metrics" is not known`) — easily mistaken for a mistake in the schema edit you just made.
**Example:** Fresh Foreman worktrees have no `node_modules`; run `yarn install --frozen-lockfile` first, then `node_modules/.bin/prisma generate --schema projects/shipping-service/prisma/schema.prisma`.

title: In Foreman worktrees, scope PR-review diffs against origin/master, not local master
content:
**Rule:** When reviewing a PR inside a Foreman worktree, derive the changed-file scope from `gh pr diff <n>` (authoritative, GitHub-computed) or `git diff origin/master...HEAD` — never `git diff master...HEAD`, because the local `master` ref is frequently stale (pinned at an old merge-base).
**When to apply:** Any review/reviewer/retry action that needs the exact set of files a PR changed, before reading the diff or dispatching scoped review agents.
**Anti-pattern:** Treating local `master` as the merge base. Two confirmed failure modes: (1) bogus file scope — on ENG-5445 local `master` lagged several merged PRs, so `git diff master...HEAD` reported 175 files / +10426 lines while the real PR was 4 files / +37; (2) phantom test failures — on ENG-5447 local `master` sat at the merge-base, so the local suite showed `runner.test.ts` failures (PWD `/var` vs `/private/var`, a flaky `checkpoint` timing case) in base code the PR never touched, which trusting local master would misattribute to the PR.
**Example:** Cross-check `gh pr diff <n> --name-only` (authoritative) against your scope before fanning out agents; run `git fetch origin master` first if origin/master itself may be behind.

title: opencode unattended runs need --dangerously-skip-permissions or the session is disposed before the final result block
content:
**Rule:** When a non-Claude runner must run unattended (Foreman workers, `foreman eval`), pass that CLI's auto-approve-permissions flag on every invocation — for opencode, `--dangerously-skip-permissions` — mirroring the claude runner.
**When to apply:** An opencode (or other unattended) runner yields no final result block / truncated final output and the `schema` grader fails with "no valid agent-result block", while the identical case passes on `--runner claude`.
**Anti-pattern:** Diagnosing it as an output-extraction/normalization bug. Extraction is fine — opencode auto-rejects any `ask`-policy permission over closed stdin (notably `external_directory`, which fires whenever cwd isn't a recognized opencode project, as in the eval's throwaway repo dir) and disposes the session before the model emits its final block.
**Example:** `foreman eval learning-policy` went 0% to 100% on the schema grader purely by adding `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to OpenCodeRunner; no output code changed.

title: Delimiter-in-payload breaks naive agent-result/judge block extraction
content:
**Rule:** When extracting a delimited JSON block whose payload can itself contain the delimiter literal (foreman's agent-result and judge tag-scans), anchor on the LAST close tag and try opening tags outermost-first (earliest then later), taking the first slice that parses as JSON; only fall back to an earlier close tag when none parse. Conversely, when emitting a worker result, avoid placing the literal delimiter string inside payload fields.
**When to apply:** Editing or adding any tag-scan that slices JSON out of open/close tags (`parseWorkerResult` in src/execution/worker-result.ts, `parseJudgeBlock` in src/eval/graders.ts), or writing a result/learning/PR body whose text describes the agent-result mechanism.
**Anti-pattern:** A naive backward scan (`lastIndexOf(open, closeStart)`) latches onto a delimiter mention inside the payload, slices a truncated fragment, fails JSON.parse, moves the close pointer earlier, and silently drops a well-formed block — failing the whole attempt. The same hazard bites a worker that emits the literal delimiter in its own payload while the deployed orchestrator is still an unfixed build, which drops the worker's own result. `parseJudgeBlock` still carries the latent read-side bug.
**Example:** ENG-5450 — a reviewer's learningMutations[].content mentioned the agent-result tag twice, so the unfixed parseWorkerResult threw the no-valid-block error on an otherwise correct result.

title: Prisma @default belongs in the domain, not the schema — and how to retire one safely
content:
**Rule:** Keep `@default(...)` out of Prisma schema models — the domain entity's `create` factory owns defaults. Never add `@default` to a new column. To retire an existing one: add the default to the domain `create` (mirroring the old schema value and widening the optional create-props set), confirm every insert path supplies the value explicitly, then drop it with a NEW migration (`ALTER COLUMN ... DROP DEFAULT`) — never edit a migration that already ran on DEV/STAGE/PROD.
**When to apply:** Adding a column to a Prisma model, or cleaning up existing `@default`s, in a Lynk DDD service (shipping, sales, warehousing, accounts…).
**Anti-pattern:** Leaving defaults in the DB schema splits business rules across two layers; silently dropping a default without a domain fallback changes behaviour for any insert path that relied on the DB default.
**Example:** ENG-5445 removed six `CarrierService` `@default`s — dropping them makes the fields required in `Prisma.CarrierServiceUncheckedCreateInput`, so `typecheck` pinpoints every insert site that had relied on the DB default. Use typecheck as the safety net before relying on the domain default alone.

title: A red Deploy-stage check on a merge commit does not mean the change failed to ship
content:
**Rule:** Don't conclude a merged change failed to ship from a FAILURE on its own merge commit's Deploy-stage check. Corroborate with (1) the change being present and unreverted in current `master`, and (2) evidence of later successful deploys stacked on top. For schema/migration work this is decisive: a failed Prisma `migrate deploy` blocks every subsequent deploy, so continued master progress proves the migration applied.
**When to apply:** Consolidating or deploy-verifying merged work whose merge-commit CircleCI Deploy stage shows FAILURE — especially days after merge, when later commits have re-run the pipeline on cumulative state.
**Anti-pattern:** Flagging a false regression (or re-triggering deploys) off a stale per-commit check when the change demonstrably shipped via cumulative later deploys.
**Example:** ENG-5445 / PR #596 merge commit showed `ci/circleci: Deploy stage` FAILURE (build 25730), yet the migration + schema change were in master with ~11 days of subsequent merges/deploys — shipped, not broken. A concurrent `Release libs` red was also unrelated, since the PR touched only the service, not the published libs.

title: Dropping a narrowing filter from a Linear query turns its `first: N` cap into silent truncation
content:
**Rule:** When you remove a narrowing filter (e.g. labels) from a Linear GraphQL query so it now fetches a broad collection, treat the existing `first: N` cap as a silent-truncation bug — paginate, or at minimum log a warning when a page returns at the cap.
**When to apply:** Reusing or adding a Linear query that fetches an unscoped/weakly-scoped set (every issue assigned to a user, every issue in a team) where the prior caller relied on a label/state filter to keep results small.
**Anti-pattern:** Reusing a `first: 250` query that was safe for a label-narrowed candidate set against the full assigned set — past the cap, issues vanish from the UI with no signal (the pilot workspace already had 93 assigned issues, approaching 250).
**Example:** `queryAssignedIssues` (`src/tasking/impl/linear-task-system.ts`) now `logger.warn`s when `data.issues.nodes.length >= 250`, tagged with team + `labelFiltered`; full pagination left as a follow-up.

title: Build a merge/dedup key set from the full in-scope collection, not a limit/search-windowed subset
content:
**Rule:** When merging two sources under a precedence rule ("source A wins on overlap"), build the dedup key set from the complete in-scope set of A, not from a subset already narrowed by limit/search/pagination — otherwise items outside that window fall through and get served from source B, losing A's authoritative state.
**When to apply:** Merging a persisted/local set with a live/remote set under a "local wins" (or any precedence) invariant, where the local set is queried with a limit/search window.
**Anti-pattern:** Deriving the dedup ids from the limit/search-windowed candidate set made "mirror wins on overlap" only conditionally true — a mirrored task outside that window re-appeared from the live assigned set and lost its persisted targets/jobs/reviews.
**Example:** `src/http.ts` builds `tasksById`/`mirroredIds` from `getAllMirroredTasks()` (every mirrored task) so the precedence invariant holds unconditionally.

title: Optimistic mutations must snapshot/roll back every cache key when one entity is fanned across keyed caches
content:
**Rule:** When the same entity appears in multiple React Query caches keyed by a UI parameter (scope/filter/tab), an optimistic mutation must cancel/snapshot/write/rollback against the shared key *prefix*, not just the active key — operating on one key leaves sibling caches stale, so the row flip-flops or reverts when the user switches.
**When to apply:** A toggle/edit mutates a record cached under several query keys (e.g. `[...prefix, scope]`) the user can switch between.
**Anti-pattern:** Writing the optimistic value only into the active scope's cache; the other scope keeps the old value, switching tabs shows a flip-flop, and rollback misses entries. (Related to React Query structural-sharing reseeding from a shared query.)
**Example:** `ui/src/hooks/use-foreman-tasks-query.ts` keys caches as `[...prefix, scope]`; the agent-enabled mutation does `cancelQueries`/`getQueriesData`/`setQueriesData`/rollback on `foremanTasksQueryKeyPrefix` and invalidates the prefix `onSettled`.

title: Add tests that fail when a scope-controlling branch or predicate is silently weakened
content:
**Rule:** When a code path silently controls scope/correctness and its only failure mode is at runtime — a conditionally-assembled query branch, or a regex/predicate that decides membership — add tests that would fail if the contract were loosened: assert the assembled query's omitted clause/variable, and cover near-miss negatives for predicates.
**When to apply:** A parameterized query builder with an on/off filter branch, or a regex/predicate that decides what's in-scope (which issues count as agent-tagged, which rows enter a union).
**Anti-pattern:** Testing only the happy branch — the `useLabels=false` branch of `queryAssignedIssues` had no test, so a dangling comma / stray `$labels` var-decl would only fail live against Linear; and the `agent:` fallback regex was asserted only on `agent:michael`, so loosening it to `/agent/i` would still pass while pulling `agentic`-labelled issues into scope.
**Example:** added a `describe("listAssignedIssues")` asserting the omitted `$labels` clause/variable for both assignee paths, and a `test.each` of match/near-miss pairs for the `agent:` regex.

title: When a PR grows scope mid-review, re-audit the comments its earlier commits left for the old narrow behavior
content:
**Rule:** After a PR grows beyond its original scope (especially stacked/agent PRs whose early commits called a capability "out of scope"), grep the touched files for comments/docstrings describing the now-superseded behavior and update them in the same PR.
**When to apply:** A PR's later commits implement behavior that earlier commits' comments explicitly defer or describe as not-yet-present (scope creep within one PR).
**Anti-pattern:** Leaving a "this is out of scope here" or "the API only returns X today" comment after the same PR implemented the feature or added a second code path — the comment now actively misleads the next reader, and reviewers spend a round flagging it.
**Example:** PR #101 added an `assigned` scope and the server-side agent-label add; two comments left from the narrow-scope phase (`foreman-cells.tsx`, `foreman-helpers.ts`) had to be reworded before merge.

title: Foreman PRs have no CI gate; verify locally and ignore the flaky yarn test baseline
content:
**Rule:** Verify foreman PRs locally — `yarn run check` plus `yarn workspace @foreman/ui typecheck`/`build` are the real gate; foreman ships no `.github/workflows` or `.circleci`, so PRs never receive GitHub status checks (statusCheckRollup is empty). Treat the `yarn test` suite as a known-flaky baseline, not a regression signal.
**When to apply:** Finishing, reviewing, or consolidating any foreman PR — before reporting check status, blocking on a red `yarn test`, or polling for checks that will never appear.
**Anti-pattern:** Blocking on `yarn test` failures that also reproduce on a clean tree, or waiting on non-existent PR checks. Prove a failure is pre-existing by stashing your diff and re-running on the clean baseline before calling it a regression.
**Example:** ENG-5372 (UI-only `format.ts` change) saw git-worktree 5s timeouts, foreman-version git-state flakiness, and ENOTEMPTY temp-dir races — all reproduced on the stashed baseline, none caused by the change; foreman's verification gate per the ticket was 'skip — no deployment'.

title: Size a compact number formatter's top tier to the largest total, not the typical row
content:
**Rule:** When changing or extending a compact short-number formatter (k/M/B), size the top tier to the largest aggregate/total the UI renders, not the typical per-row value — and when a unit letter would collide with an adjacent column's unit, disambiguate by casing.
**When to apply:** Editing a short-number/byte/count formatter that feeds both per-row cells and a total/aggregate row, or when two adjacent columns would show the same unit letter with different meanings.
**Anti-pattern:** Only checking typical row values, so a total row overflows the top tier and renders absurdly (e.g. `2300M` instead of `2.3B`); or reusing lowercase `m` for millions next to a duration column where `m` means minutes.
**Example:** ENG-5372 added a `B` tier to `formatShortNumber` because the Usage Total row aggregates cache-read tokens (which 'dominate volume') over a default 7-day window, and switched millions to uppercase `M` so `2.2M` tokens never reads as `3m 31s`.

title: Foreman UI: make shared data tables responsive via CSS scroll-shadow + table-fixed min-width
content:
**Rule:** To make a Foreman UI data table responsive, apply the pure-CSS `scroll-shadow-x` utility (defined in `ui/src/index.css`) to the shared `table-container` in `ui/src/components/ui/table.tsx` for a JS-free horizontal-overflow cue, and give any `table-fixed` table a `min-w-[Nrem]` so it scrolls inside the container instead of squishing columns.
**When to apply:** When a data table's columns overflow horizontally with no visible affordance, or collide/overprint at narrow (mobile) widths.
**Anti-pattern:** A `table-fixed` table with no `min-w` squishes columns until headers overprint at ~390px; bare `overflow-x-auto` hides that more columns exist to the right. Avoid JS scroll listeners — layered gradients with `background-attachment: local, local, scroll, scroll` give an edge shadow that fades in only when there is more content, and the shadow color must be theme-aware (e.g. 16% black in light vs 55% in dark).
**Example:** ENG-5370 added `scroll-shadow-x` to the shared container so one change fixed all four tables, plus `min-w-[48rem]`/`min-w-[36rem]` on the two overview `table-fixed` tables; keeping the affordance in the shared container kept per-page diffs to just the min-width.

title: Split create from detail by route/dialog — `isNew` is an anti-pattern
content:
**Rule:** In lynk-frontend, model entity creation as a separate surface (its own route, or a dialog when the designs call for it) — never an `isNew`/`id==='new'` branch on the detail page; the `use<Entity>` detail hook must not carry a create mutation, and don't introduce a `useCreate<Entity>` hook (inline the create form schema + mutation in the dialog/list, following the existing create-carrier pattern).
**When to apply:** Building or cloning a CRUD surface (list + create + detail) in lynk-frontend, especially when cloning an existing `*-details` page that uses an `id === 'new'` branch.
**Anti-pattern:** Cloning `carrier-service-details` and implementing create via `isNew` — the maintainer rejects this; it couples two distinct flows and bloats the detail hook with a create mutation.
**Example:** ENG-5326's ticket asked to clone the detail page and implement `id === 'new'`; review reshaped it into a `FormDialog` launched from the list, with the `:id` route existing-only and `isNew` removed.

title: Client-side DataTable lists must load all pages via fetchAllPages
content:
**Rule:** A client-paginated DataTable page (`manualPagination: false`) must load the complete result set first via `fetchAllPages(({ cursor, take }) => gateway.listX({ cursor, take }))`; the gateway list method therefore needs `take`/`cursor` params (mirror `getCarriers`/`getCarrierServices`).
**When to apply:** Adding or cloning any platform list page backed by a paginated backend GET that does client-side pagination/sort.
**Anti-pattern:** Calling the list gateway once and letting the client paginate over only the backend's first page — rows beyond the default page size silently never render, and totals/sort run over the truncated set.
**Example:** ENG-5326 `getCarrierAccounts` initially took no `take`/`cursor`; fixed to use `fetchAllPages`, with a browser test mocking two backend pages and asserting a second-page row renders.

title: Sequential non-idempotent POSTs in one mutationFn duplicate on retry
content:
**Rule:** When a single React Query `mutationFn` performs two sequential non-idempotent creates (create parent, then create child), make the first idempotent — cache its created id in a `useRef` and skip re-creating on retry — or split the flow so retrying the second call can't re-create the first.
**When to apply:** Any `mutationFn` chaining create-A then create-B where B can fail independently and the user can retry from the same form.
**Anti-pattern:** Re-running both creates on retry — the already-succeeded parent is created a second time and the first is orphaned (often with no delete endpoint to clean it up).
**Example:** ENG-5326 create-account-then-brackets created duplicate accounts on retry until the account id was cached in a ref (later moot once create became a dialog separate from bracket save).

title: Keep entity detail hooks minimal; push derivations down; setQueryData over invalidate
content:
**Rule:** A `use<Entity>` detail hook should expose only the entity, its joined entities, and directly-related queries/mutations — push sibling-entity queries and derived values (counts, filtered option lists) down into the consuming card/dialog closest to usage; for save mutations, write the persisted record into the cache with `setQueryData` rather than `invalidateQueries`.
**When to apply:** Writing or reviewing an entity detail hook that is accumulating extra queries or derived state, or a save mutation that invalidates.
**Anti-pattern:** A detail hook that also loads sibling collections and computes `xCount`/`availableX` for child components, or invalidates after save (refetch flash; tempts create-then-update dedupe hacks).
**Example:** ENG-5326 trimmed `useCarrierAccount` to entity + joined carrier + brackets; service-count moved to `CarrierSummaryCard`, available-services to `AddCarrierServiceDialog`; `saveBrackets` switched to `setQueryData`.

title: Don't gate routes/nav behind local-only feature flags — omit the nav item instead
content:
**Rule:** In lynk-frontend, don't add feature-flag plumbing to routes or nav for not-yet-ready features; the established practice is to simply not add the nav item until it's ready (the route may render unconditionally — users can't reach it without a UI link, and it stays testable by direct URL).
**When to apply:** Shipping a new page that isn't ready for general access, or when tempted to mirror simulation-ui's flag-gated routes.
**Anti-pattern:** Adding `useFeatureFlags` route gates / `NavSubItem` flag plumbing for local-only flags — extra plumbing for no real gating benefit.
**Example:** ENG-5326's ticket asked for a flag mirroring simulation-ui; review had it dropped — nav item omitted, route unconditional.

title: Storybook/test gateway mocks must return the full persisted DTO
content:
**Rule:** Mock gateway create/update methods in stories and tests to return the full persisted DTO (mirror the real `data.<entity>` envelope), not the request body echoed back; cache-merge logic (`setQueryData` keyed on `id`) depends on the response carrying `id` and all server-populated fields.
**When to apply:** Writing Storybook stories or browser tests for pages whose save flow merges the mutation result into the query cache.
**Anti-pattern:** Echoing the request body (e.g. `{ bracketsConfig }`) as the saved record — missing `id`/foreign keys make the cache merge append an orphan row that can slip past null-filters and crash sorts (`Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'localeCompare')`).
**Example:** ENG-5326 story mocks echoed the update body; fixed to return the full `carrierWeightBracket` DTO, eliminating the Storybook row-update crash the maintainer found.

title: Verify deferred multi-part ticket items actually landed before trusting Done
content:
**Rule:** When consolidating a multi-part ticket, independently verify in the merged base branch that every deliberately-deferred or "reconciliation" follow-up item actually landed — never infer it from the task's Done state.
**When to apply:** The ticket describes coupled changes where one part was pushed to a follow-up, or a comment flags an item "left open / holding until confirmed," and the task later reached a terminal/Done state.
**Anti-pattern:** Treating Done as proof the deferred change shipped. Task state can advance independently of code, and a later unrelated commit touching the same file (e.g. bumping a "last verified" header) can make the area look maintained while the deferred fix is still missing.
**Example:** ENG-5292 shipped the Opus 4.8 default (PR #83) but the flagged claude-opus-4-7 rate correction (15/75 -> 5/25) never landed; rates.ts on master still read 15/75 even after ENG-5391's 2026-06-10 "last verified" bump, yet the issue was Done.

title: Don't rely on fire-and-forget (void promise) after the response in Lambda handlers
content:
**Rule:** Don't dispatch `void somePromise()` work that must complete after an async Lambda handler has already returned its response — run the side effect synchronously inside the request flow, or decouple it to a separate handler via EventBridge/SQS.
**When to apply:** Adding shadow comparisons, instrumentation, audit logging, or any best-effort side effect from inside a Lambda-backed request path (quote, API handler, event consumer).
**Anti-pattern:** Relying on the container draining the event loop after the response (`callbackWaitsForEmptyEventLoop`) to finish the work. That flag does not apply to async handlers and is unsupported on newer AWS Node runtimes (Node 24+); the promise can be frozen or killed mid-flight, silently dropping events and skewing any metric built on them.
**Example:** ENG-5266 dropped a planned read-path `void runShadowCompare(...)` after a maintainer flagged exactly this, folding the comparison synchronously into the existing write path instead.

title: Error-isolation try/catch around Result-returning calls must also branch on .isSad()
content:
**Rule:** When wrapping a `Result`-returning gateway/repo call for error isolation (instrumentation, fire-and-forget, best-effort paths), handle BOTH thrown exceptions (try/catch) AND the `Result.failure` / `.isSad()` branch explicitly — they are separate failure channels.
**When to apply:** Writing best-effort or isolated code that calls a Lynk gateway/repo and is meant to log-and-continue on failure.
**Anti-pattern:** A try/catch alone. Lynk gateways/repos return `Result.failure` instead of throwing for the common error case (non-2xx, parse error), so the catch never fires; the sad result becomes a null/empty value that downstream code skips silently — no error log, and any match-rate/denominator metric is quietly understated.
**Example:** ENG-5266's first shadow impl only logged on thrown errors; the gateway `Result.failure` path dropped the required `*_shadow_compare_error` event until a dedicated `.isSad()` branch + sibling test were added.

title: Place cache shadow-compare where both values coexist for the current rollout phase
content:
**Rule:** Before instrumenting a cache shadow/validation compare, confirm which code path actually holds both the fresh and cached values in the *current* rollout phase, and instrument there — don't assume the read path.
**When to apply:** Adding shadow/drift validation during a phased cache enablement (e.g. CarrierRate cache: write-only -> production-usable).
**Anti-pattern:** Instrumenting the cache-HIT read path while the cache is still write-only in prod (`CARRIER_RATE_CACHE_PRODUCTION_USABLE` off) — there are no production read hits yet, so the shadow never fires. In the write-only phase the only place a fresh gateway value and an existing cached row coexist is the upsert/write path (`cacheGatewayRates`); compare gateway-response-about-to-be-cached vs existing row there — no extra gateway call, no added quote latency.
**Example:** ENG-5266 shipped `carrier_rate_cache_shadow_compare` (matched/diverged) from the write path gated on `CARRIER_RATE_CACHE_SHADOW_COMPARE`, not the planned read-path fire-and-forget.

title: ddd-core root barrel's browser-unsafety comes from logging-client's eager AsyncLocalStorage, not ddd-core source
content:
**Rule:** Before implementing a "make X browser-safe / lazily construct a Node-only API" ticket, confirm the named file and symbol actually exist (`git log --all -S <symbol>` + `rg`), then trace the *actual* root-barrel import graph into `node_modules` to locate the eager construction — it may live in a dependency, not the named file.

**When to apply:** Any ticket prescribing a specific file/symbol fix for a module-load crash (e.g. `AsyncLocalStorage is not a constructor`) in `@invenco/ddd-core` or a similar small barrel library.

**Anti-pattern:** Implementing the prescribed one-file change blindly. In ddd-core there is no `repository/OperationTerminator.ts` and no `new AsyncLocalStorage()` (never has been, per full git history). The eager construction is in `@invenco/logging-client@0.3.1` (`lib/asyncLocalStorage.js:5`), reached via `src/domain/events/DomainEvents.ts`'s top-level `import { getLogger } from '@invenco/logging-client'`. Fixing it requires the logging-client package (then a ddd-core dep bump), not a ddd-core source edit.

**Example:** `rg "new AsyncLocalStorage" node_modules` -> the only hit is `@invenco/logging-client/lib/asyncLocalStorage.js:5`.
